patterns-navigation-pagination--docs.name: Docs
patterns-navigation-pagination--docs.title: Patterns/Navigation/Pagination
patterns-navigation-pagination--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Pagination.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Controlled, one-based pagination across a known page count with localised controls, boundaries, and compact ellipses."
/>

# Pagination

`Pagination` requests movement through a known, one-based page range while the application owns data and route state.

## Import

```tsx
import { Pagination } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it when a result set has a stable, known `pageCount` and direct page access is useful.

## When not to use

Do not use it for an unknown total, continuous scrolling, or collections where retaining nearby items is more useful than moving between discrete pages. Prefer an application-owned “Load more” action when the total is unknown, or the virtualisation support in `Table`, `GridList`, and `ListBox` for large scrolling collections.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Pagination } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Pagination
  aria-label="Search result pages"
  currentPage={4}
  onChange={(page) => {
    setPage(page);
    loadResults({ page });
  }}
  pageCount={12}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ManyPages} />

## State, values, and callbacks

Pagination is controlled. `currentPage` and every value passed to `onChange(page)` are one-based positive integers. The callback reports the requested page and receives no DOM event; update route and data state, then pass the resulting `currentPage` back. There is no uncontrolled `defaultCurrentPage` or read-only state.

`pageCount` must be a positive integer. `currentPage` must be an integer from `1` through `pageCount`, inclusive. Invalid values throw `RangeError` during rendering instead of being clamped. Long ranges always keep first, last, current, and adjacent pages available, with non-interactive ellipses for gaps. The current page is a non-interactive element with `aria-current="page"`, so selecting it cannot trigger a redundant request.

`disabled` defaults to `false` and prevents all page-change controls. Previous and next are also disabled at their respective boundaries. Disabling does not change or hide the current page.

## Data, errors, and routing

The application owns fetching, cancellation, caching, URLs, scroll restoration, focus after navigation, loading, empty results, and errors. Keep the previous page visible while a request is pending and set `disabled`, or replace the results with suitable progress feedback. Do not change `currentPage` to an invalid value when a result count shrinks; resolve the valid page in application state first.

Pagination renders buttons, not links, and does not call the router adapter. When every page needs a crawlable or open-in-new-tab URL, compose an application-specific navigation solution from public link primitives.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

The required `aria-label` distinguishes this `nav` landmark from primary navigation. The current page receives the provider's localised `page` label; directional buttons use `previousPage` and `nextPage`. Override those messages through `BreezeProvider` when localising the application.

Controls follow normal button keyboard behaviour: `Tab` visits enabled buttons and `Enter` or `Space` activates them. There is no roving focus or arrow-key page selection. Ellipses are hidden from assistive technology. In right-to-left direction, previous and next arrow icons reverse while callback values remain numerically unchanged.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass zero-based indexes, set `pageCount` to zero for empty results, treat the callback as a DOM event, use pagination for an unknown total, or announce every pending request by replacing the landmark. Related components: [Table](?path=/docs/data-display-table--docs), [GridList](?path=/docs/collections-gridlist--docs), [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).
patterns-navigation-pagination--docs.summary: Controlled, one-based pagination across a known page count with localised controls, boundaries, and compact ellipses.

patterns-forms-passwordfield--docs.name: Docs
patterns-forms-passwordfield--docs.title: Patterns/Forms/PasswordField
patterns-forms-passwordfield--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './PasswordField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled password-entry pattern with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only string state, semantic change callbacks, validation anatomy and a provider-localised keyboard-operable visibility action."
/>

# PasswordField

`PasswordField` provides labelled password entry with supporting guidance, validation feedback and a show-or-hide action.

## Import

```tsx
import { PasswordField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `PasswordField` for a current or new password when the canonical label, input, description, error and visibility composition is sufficient.

## When not to use

Use compound `TextField` with `type="password"` when you need native attributes or composition points that PasswordField does not expose. Use a normal `TextField` for non-secret text. PasswordField does not validate password strength, compare fields, generate passwords, store credentials or submit forms.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { PasswordField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

export function NewPasswordField() {
  const [password, setPassword] = useState('');

  return (
    <PasswordField
      autoComplete="new-password"
      description="Use at least 12 characters."
      label="New password"
      name="new-password"
      onChange={setPassword}
      required
      value={password}
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Visibility} />

## Anatomy

PasswordField is a fixed pattern rather than a public compound namespace.

| Region            | Responsibility                                                          |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Label             | Required persistent accessible name for the native input.               |
| Input group       | One visual surface containing the password input and visibility action. |
| Visibility action | Toggles the native input between `type="password"` and `type="text"`.   |
| Description       | Optional persistent requirements or guidance associated with the input. |
| Error             | Optional validation message associated while the field is invalid.      |

Visibility is internal presentation state and always starts hidden. It does not alter the password string. Read-only values may still be revealed; disabled fields disable both editing and the visibility action.

## Value state and callbacks

- **Uncontrolled:** omit `value`; `defaultValue` defaults to an empty string. Optional `onChange` observes edits.
- **Controlled:** provide `value` and `onChange`. The callback receives the complete next password string, never a DOM event.
- **Read-only:** provide `value` with `readOnly`; omit `onChange` and `defaultValue`.

Controlled and uncontrolled props are mutually exclusive. Mutable controlled state requires `onChange`; PasswordField throws if JavaScript bypasses that public TypeScript contract. The application owns password rules, strength calculations, matching, submission, server errors and security handling.

## Sizes, validation and browser behaviour

`size` accepts `'sm'`, `'md'` or `'lg'` and defaults to `'md'`; it sizes both the input and visibility action. Appearance and semantic colour are fixed.

Set `invalid` with meaningful `error` copy to expose invalid state and its associated message. `required` supplies native and accessible required state but does not invent a business rule. `description` remains visible for persistent requirements. `placeholder` is optional example text and never replaces `label`.

Use `autoComplete="current-password"` for an existing credential and `autoComplete="new-password"` for creation or reset. `'off'` is supported but browsers and password managers may choose their own security behaviour. `name` participates in native submission. There is no loading or empty-content mode; use the empty string as the empty value and keep pending submission state on the form action.

## Keyboard, accessibility and internationalisation

- The native input supports ordinary text-entry, selection, copy and paste keyboard behaviour. Tab moves to the visibility button; Enter or Space toggles visibility.
- The visibility icon is decorative. Its required accessible label switches between the provider messages `showPassword` and `hidePassword`; configure those messages for the application locale.
- Keep `label` visible and translate label, guidance and validation copy. Password values are opaque strings and are never translated or reformatted.
- Provider direction is inherited by the field. Do not alter or reverse a password based on writing direction.
- Disabling prevents focus and editing. Read-only preserves focus and copying; explain immutable state when it is not obvious.

PasswordField performs no routing. It exposes no custom class, ref, id, input mode, length constraints, pattern, ARIA attribute pass-through or custom visibility icon; compose `TextField`, `InputGroup` and `IconButton` directly when those deliberate public capabilities are needed.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not put strength instructions only in a placeholder, use `invalid` without useful error copy, log password values in callbacks, disable password-manager support without a concrete reason, or assume the visibility toggle changes value state. Use `TextField` for custom password anatomy and `FormSection` or `FormActions` for surrounding form structure.
patterns-forms-passwordfield--docs.summary: Labelled password-entry pattern with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only string state, semantic change callbacks, validation anatomy and a provider-localised keyboard-operable visibility action.

patterns-page-structure-sectionheader--d.title: Patterns/Page Structure/SectionHeader
patterns-page-structure-sectionheader--d.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './SectionHeader.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact content-section header with a fixed level-two heading, supporting description, and one directly related action."
/>

# SectionHeader

`SectionHeader` aligns a section's `h2`, concise supporting context, and one related action.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, SectionHeader } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it at the start of a major region within a page when a visible boundary and one action clarify the section. Use `PageHeader` for the page's `h1` and page-level actions, `Card.Header` for content inside a card, or `Typography` when only a heading is needed.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, SectionHeader } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<section aria-labelledby="recent-updates-heading">
  <SectionHeader
    action={<Button appearance="ghost">View all updates</Button>}
    description="Latest changes across the workspace"
    id="recent-updates-heading"
    title="Recent updates"
  />
  {/* Section content */}
</section>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.WithAction} />

## Structure and composition

The pattern renders a semantic `header`, a fixed `h2`, an optional paragraph, and an optional action wrapper. `id` is assigned to the `h2`, not to the root header, so a surrounding region can reference it with `aria-labelledby`.

The action does not wrap and the title column shrinks. Prefer one concise low-emphasis control. Use `ButtonGroup` or a page-level action area when several controls are necessary, and test translated copy at compact widths.

## Accessibility, state, and internationalisation

Keep the document outline logical: because the title is always `h2`, use another composition when the region requires a deeper heading level. The visible title should identify the following region; provide a unique `id` when another element references it. The optional action must have its own accessible name and must make sense in relation to this section.

`SectionHeader` adds no keyboard interaction. The slotted action retains its own focus, disabled, loading, and activation semantics. The pattern has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, invalid, empty, loading, or error state; present content states below it, or use `StatePanel` when the entire region is replaced.

Translate `title`, `description`, and action copy. Logical flex alignment supports left-to-right and right-to-left direction without reversing application-owned content order.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use it as the page `h1`, assign its `id` expecting the root header to receive it, fit a toolbar into the single-action slot, or let action text obscure the heading at narrow widths. Related components: [PageHeader](?path=/docs/patterns-page-structure-pageheader--docs), [Card](?path=/docs/data-display-card--docs), [Typography](?path=/docs/foundation-typography--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs.name: Docs
patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs.title: Patterns/Feedback/StatePanel
patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './StatePanel.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Complete contextual panel for an empty, error, success, warning, or informational region with guidance and optional recovery."
/>

# StatePanel

`StatePanel` replaces a substantial content region with a clear state, explanation, and optional next step.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, StatePanel } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { InfoIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use it for an empty view, failed region, completed setup, blocked state, or substantial informational state. Use `Alert` for inline feedback that should remain beside content, `Toast` for transient queued feedback, `Skeleton` or `Spinner` for pending work, and a field's `Error` part for validation.

The application owns state detection, retries, permissions, analytics, and domain copy. The pattern does not infer state from data or perform recovery.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, StatePanel } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { InfoIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

<StatePanel
  action={<Button onAction={() => createItem()}>Create item</Button>}
  description="Create the first item to begin using this screen."
  icon={<InfoIcon />}
  title="No items yet"
  variant="info"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Empty} />

## Anatomy, variants, and state

The root is a `section` labelled by its generated `h2`. A large decorative `IconTile` sits beside the title and description; the optional action moves below the copy and stretches on compact screens. There are no public compound parts.

`variant` controls semantic marker colour and defaults to `info`:

| Variant   | Typical use                                            |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `info`    | Neutral guidance or an empty state.                    |
| `primary` | A constructive product next step.                      |
| `success` | A completed outcome that deserves a full-region state. |
| `warning` | A condition requiring care before proceeding.          |
| `danger`  | A failure or blocked condition requiring recovery.     |

Colour does not set announcement behaviour or application status. Use explicit title and description copy. The pattern has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, loading, or invalid props. Disable or load the slotted action itself. For pending content, prefer progress feedback rather than describing loading as an error state.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

`title` supplies the region's accessible name and must identify the actual state. The icon tile is decorative by default; do not rely on its shape, icon, or colour to communicate meaning. Keep guidance actionable and put one clear recovery or next-step control in `action`.

The panel is not a live region by default. For newly inserted urgent errors, choose `Alert` or deliberately add an appropriate native `role`/`aria-live` attribute only when immediate announcement is required; avoid repeated announcements during re-renders. The panel itself has no keyboard interaction, while the slotted action retains its own keyboard and callback semantics.

Translate all visible copy. Logical grid placement and alignment follow provider direction. Choose direction-neutral icons where possible; application-authored directional icons must account for right-to-left presentation.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use `danger` colour as the only error signal, place several competing actions in the slot, show an empty-state panel while data is merely pending, or assume insertion is announced. Related components: [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs), [Skeleton](?path=/docs/feedback-skeleton--docs), [Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs), and [IconTile](?path=/docs/foundation-icontile--docs).
patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs.summary: Complete contextual panel for an empty, error, success, warning, or informational region with guidance and optional recovery.

patterns-application-shell-usermenu--doc.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './UserMenu.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Application-shell account menu combining represented-user identity, optional notifications, and keyed actions or destinations."
/>

# UserMenu

`UserMenu` presents the represented user and a concise set of account actions in a keyboard-complete menu.

## Import

```tsx
import { UserMenu } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { SignOutIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use it in `ApplicationShell` for identity, a short notification summary, account destinations, and actions such as signing out.

The application owns authentication, notification fetching and read state, confirmation, errors, analytics, and navigation. `UserMenu` only composes presentation and menu behaviour.

## When not to use

Do not use it as a general command menu, primary navigation, or a substantial notification centre. Prefer `Menu` for unrelated commands, `NavigationList` for persistent destinations, and a dedicated page for a complete notification history.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { UserMenu } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { SignOutIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

<UserMenu
  aria-label="User menu"
  actions={[
    { href: '/profile', id: 'profile', label: 'Profile' },
    {
      icon: <SignOutIcon />,
      id: 'sign-out',
      label: 'Sign out',
      onAction: () => signOut(),
      variant: 'danger',
    },
  ]}
  hasUnread
  notificationHeading="Notifications"
  notificationState="1 new"
  notifications="Your export is ready."
  userName="Taylor Reed"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.NotificationsAndActions} />

## Actions, routing, and state

`actions` is an ordered collection. Every action requires a stable string `id` and plain-string `label`; `label` is visible and drives typeahead. `onAction(id)` receives that action's id after semantic activation, not a DOM event. `href` makes an item a destination: local `/path` values use the `BreezeProvider` router adapter when available, while external URLs and native link behaviour remain native. An item may have an action, a destination, or both when activation intentionally performs both responsibilities.

`disabled` prevents an action from receiving focus or activation. `variant="danger"` provides danger emphasis; `default` is the default. Use danger only for genuinely destructive or terminating actions and provide confirmation separately when needed.

`hasUnread` defaults to `false`. When true, the trigger gets a visible decorative dot and its accessible name appends the provider's `unreadNotifications` message. The pattern does not mark notifications read. Notification content is rendered only when `notifications` is supplied; the heading and state are optional supporting content.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

`aria-label` must identify the trigger without relying on the visible user name. `userName` names the avatar and is also visible in the full shell presentation. Provide a meaningful image in `src` or allow `Avatar` to derive a fallback. Action icons are decorative because their labels carry meaning.

The trigger opens with pointer activation, `Enter`, `Space`, or the platform menu shortcut. Arrow keys move between enabled actions, typing searches labels, `Enter` or `Space` activates, `Home` and `End` move to boundaries, and `Escape` closes and restores focus. Logical alignment and `top start` placement follow provider direction.

Translate the accessible label, notification copy, user-facing state, and action labels. `Unread notifications` comes from `BreezeProvider.messages.unreadNotifications`. Keep labels concise enough for narrow shell presentation.

There is no root loading, invalid, empty, disabled, controlled-open, or error state. Avoid opening an empty menu; render account-loading feedback outside it, disable individual actions during in-flight work, and show any failure in an application-owned alert or page region.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass domain objects as ids, use a danger style for ordinary navigation, treat `hasUnread` as application state storage, or place a long notification feed in the popover. Related components: [ApplicationShell](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-applicationshell--docs), [Avatar](?path=/docs/foundation-avatar--docs), [Menu](?path=/docs/navigation-menu--docs), and [ConfirmationDialog](?path=/docs/patterns-actions-confirmationdialog--docs).

primitives-navigation-accordion--docs.name: Docs
primitives-navigation-accordion--docs.title: Primitives/Navigation/Accordion
primitives-navigation-accordion--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Accordion.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A coordinated group of keyed disclosures with single or multiple expansion and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, and disabled states."
/>

# Accordion

`Accordion` organises related sections into labelled panels whose expansion is coordinated by one root.

## Import

```tsx
import { Accordion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it to reduce the initial length of supporting content while keeping several related headings visible. Use `Disclosure` for one independent section, `Tabs` when exactly one peer view should be active, and ordinary headings when all content should remain visible or searchable at once.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Accordion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Accordion.Root defaultValue={['delivery']}>
  <Accordion.Item id="delivery">
    <Accordion.Trigger>Delivery</Accordion.Trigger>
    <Accordion.Panel>Delivery details</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
  <Accordion.Item id="returns">
    <Accordion.Trigger>Returns</Accordion.Trigger>
    <Accordion.Panel>Returns details</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SingleUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy

| Part                | Purpose                                                       |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Accordion.Root`    | Owns expansion keys and the single/multiple expansion policy. |
| `Accordion.Item`    | One stable keyed disclosure.                                  |
| `Accordion.Trigger` | Heading button that toggles its item.                         |
| `Accordion.Panel`   | Collapsible content associated with the trigger.              |

`Trigger` must precede its associated `Panel` inside each item. `headingLevel` defaults to `3`; choose the level that preserves the page heading hierarchy. A panel defaults to group semantics and may use `role="region"` when it is important enough to be a landmark.

## Expansion and states

`value` and `defaultValue` are arrays of complete `string | number` item ids. Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled expansion, `value` with `onChange` for controlled expansion, or `value` with `readOnly` for immutable expansion. `onChange` receives the complete next array. `multiple` defaults to `false`; when true, items expand independently.

Root `disabled` disables every trigger; item `disabled` disables only one. Disabled open content remains readable. Accordion has no loading, invalid, empty, or error state: render feedback inside a panel, or use `StatePanel` instead of an empty accordion.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and styling

Parts accept `className`, relevant semantic attributes, and refs to their rendered elements. The Trigger supports relevant native button attributes; Breeze owns click, disabled, and inline style behaviour.

## Keyboard, accessibility, internationalisation, and mistakes

Tab visits each enabled trigger; Enter or Space toggles it. Focus does not rove with arrow keys. The trigger exposes expanded state and its panel relationship. Keep trigger text meaningful when heard alone, avoid putting controls inside it, and let translated headings wrap. Logical text alignment follows direction; ids remain stable.

Do not use array positions as ids, combine `value` with `defaultValue`, use `multiple` when content order implies a single choice, or hide essential primary content solely to shorten a page. Related components: `Disclosure`, `Tabs`, `Stepper`, and `NavigationList`.
primitives-navigation-accordion--docs.summary: A coordinated group of keyed disclosures with single or multiple expansion and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, and disabled states.

feedback-alert--docs.name: Docs
feedback-alert--docs.title: Feedback/Alert
feedback-alert--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Alert.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Persistent inline feedback with an explicit assertive, polite, or non-live announcement policy."
/>

# Alert

`Alert` displays persistent inline feedback and makes announcement urgency an explicit choice.

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use an alert for feedback that should remain in the page near the affected context. Use [Toast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs) for transient, provider-queued notifications, [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs) for a substantial empty or error state, and field `Error` parts for validation attached to a control.

Do not use an assertive alert for ordinary guidance already present at page load.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SaveFeedback() {
  return (
    <Alert announcement="polite" variant="success">
      Your changes were saved.
    </Alert>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.InformationNotice} />

## Announcement behaviour

- `assertive` is the default. It renders `role="alert"`, `aria-live="assertive"`, and `aria-atomic="true"` for urgent newly inserted feedback.
- `polite` renders `role="status"`, `aria-live="polite"`, and `aria-atomic="true"` for non-urgent updates.
- `off` omits the live-region role and sets `aria-live="off"`; use it for persistent content already present when the page loads.

The DOM change must occur after the live region exists for reliable announcements. Do not duplicate the same message in another live region.

## Variants

`variant` accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark`; the default is `info`. Choose by meaning, not decoration, and include text or an accessible icon so colour is never the only signal.

<Canvas of={Stories.Variants} />

## Accessibility, state, and direction

Alert has no keyboard interaction or internal controlled state. It does not own dismissal, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error logic; conditionally render it from application state and use `danger` for error meaning when appropriate. Translate its application-owned content. Its logical layout supports both directions; decorative icons should be hidden from assistive technology and meaningful icons need a text alternative without repeating the message.

`Alert` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `role` and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not leave `announcement="assertive"` on static informational content.
- Do not announce a validation error both in `Alert` and a field error.
- Do not put the only dismiss or recovery action in non-interactive text.
- Do not choose `success`, `warning`, or `danger` solely for visual emphasis.

## Related components

[Toast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs), [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs), and compound field error parts cover transient feedback, page states, and validation.
feedback-alert--docs.summary: Persistent inline feedback with an explicit assertive, polite, or non-live announcement policy.

primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs.name: Docs
primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs.title: Primitives/Overlays/AlertDialog
primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './AlertDialog.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A modal alert requiring an explicit, clearly labelled decision with protected outside dismissal, contained focus, and responsive action placement."
/>

# AlertDialog

`AlertDialog` interrupts the user with a consequential decision that requires an explicit response.

## Import

```tsx
import { AlertDialog } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it before an irreversible or materially risky action, or when an interruption cannot be ignored. Use `Dialog` for ordinary modal tasks, `ConfirmationDialog` for the standard composed confirmation pattern, `Alert` for inline feedback, and `Toast` for non-blocking status.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { AlertDialog } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<AlertDialog.Root>
  <AlertDialog.Trigger variant="danger">Delete item</AlertDialog.Trigger>
  <AlertDialog.Content>
    <AlertDialog.Title>Delete this item?</AlertDialog.Title>
    <AlertDialog.Description>
      This action cannot be undone.
    </AlertDialog.Description>
    <AlertDialog.Actions>
      <AlertDialog.Close appearance="outline" autoFocus>
        Cancel
      </AlertDialog.Close>
      <AlertDialog.Close variant="danger" onAction={deleteItem}>
        Delete permanently
      </AlertDialog.Close>
    </AlertDialog.Actions>
  </AlertDialog.Content>
</AlertDialog.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ExplicitDecision} />

## Anatomy

| Part                      | Purpose                                                        |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AlertDialog.Root`        | Coordinates open state and trigger focus.                      |
| `AlertDialog.Trigger`     | Opens the alert dialog.                                        |
| `AlertDialog.Content`     | Non-outside-dismissible `alertdialog` surface and focus scope. |
| `AlertDialog.Title`       | Required concise decision heading and accessible name.         |
| `AlertDialog.Description` | Required consequence explanation and accessible description.   |
| `AlertDialog.Actions`     | Responsive group for explicit choices.                         |
| `AlertDialog.Close`       | Runs an optional action and closes the alert dialog.           |

Put every decision in `Actions`. On compact layouts actions fill the width and visual order places the destructive choice above cancel while DOM and keyboard order remain logical. Put the safest initial focus on Cancel with `autoFocus`; do not automatically focus a destructive action.

## State and dismissal

Use `defaultOpen`, controlled `open` with `onOpenChange`, or immutable `open` with `readOnly`. `onOpenChange` receives the complete next Boolean. Outside pointer interaction never dismisses AlertDialog. Escape dismisses by default; `keyboardDismissDisabled` removes that path, so an enabled explicit action is mandatory.

Loading, disabled, invalid, empty, and error behaviour belongs to the decision actions or content. A loading destructive action should remain clearly labelled and must not leave the dialog with no usable cancellation path.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and callback semantics

Use `onAction`, not a DOM click callback. `Actions` supports relevant `div` attributes; title, description, and button parts support their relevant native attributes. Breeze owns the alert-dialog role, focus scope, and inline styling.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and mistakes

Trigger activation opens the alert and focus stays contained with Tab and Shift+Tab. Escape closes unless explicitly disabled; an explicit Close restores trigger focus. State the action and consequence in plain language, use specific action labels rather than “Yes” and “No”, and never rely on danger colour alone. Translate all decision copy together and allow wrapping.

Do not use AlertDialog for success messages, routine acknowledgements, or long forms; do not make outside click the only cancellation path; do not omit Title or Description. Related components: `Dialog`, `ConfirmationDialog`, `Alert`, and `Toast`.
primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs.summary: A modal alert requiring an explicit, clearly labelled decision with protected outside dismissal, contained focus, and responsive action placement.

foundation-avatar--docs.name: Docs
foundation-avatar--docs.title: Foundation/Avatar
foundation-avatar--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Avatar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible person or entity representation with a named image and deterministic initials fallback."
/>

# Avatar

`Avatar` represents a named person or entity with an optional image and a deterministic initials fallback.

## Import

```tsx
import { Avatar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use a circular avatar for a person in account, participant, or authorship context.
- Use a square avatar for a compact non-person entity marker.
- Supply `src` only when an image is available; the same component handles loading failure by falling back to initials.

## When not to use

- Use `Logo` for the canonical Motech Development brand mark.
- Use `IconTile` for a semantic icon or short status marker that does not represent identity.
- Use ordinary content and `Typography` when the full name is the primary information; an Avatar should not replace necessary visible identity text.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Avatar, Inline, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function PersonSummary() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Avatar name="Alex Morgan" />
      <Typography as="span">Alex Morgan</Typography>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Initials} />

## Image and fallback behaviour

When `src` is present, Avatar renders a cover-cropped image. The nested image has empty alternative text because the wrapper already exposes `name` once as a named image. If the resource fails to load, Avatar displays initials without changing its accessible name.

Without an `initials` override, Breeze trims `name`, takes the first character of the first two whitespace-separated words, uppercases them, and joins them. For example, “Alex Morgan” becomes “AM”. An explicit `initials` value is useful for an established short form or compact entity marker.

```tsx
import { Avatar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function EntityMarker() {
  return (
    <Avatar
      initials="EX"
      name="Example organisation"
      shape="square"
      tone="accent"
    />
  );
}
```

## Shape, size, and tone

- `circle` is the default and is preferred for people.
- `square` has square corners and is preferred for non-person entity markers.
- `sm` and `md` both occupy a 36-pixel square; `sm` uses smaller initials. `lg` occupies a 56-pixel square.
- `primary` is the default semantic treatment. `accent` provides a distinct alternate entity-marker treatment.

Tone applies to the initials fallback. A successfully loaded image fills the available geometry.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, state, and internationalisation

Relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `aria-describedby`, and `data-*` are forwarded. `children` and arbitrary `style` are excluded so the image/fallback anatomy and canonical appearance remain stable.

Avatar exposes `role="img"` with `name` as its accessible name. Always provide the complete human-readable name even when visible initials are overridden. Place visible text beside the Avatar when users need to identify or distinguish similar people and entities; the accessible name alone is not visible context.

Avatar has no keyboard interaction and is not an action. Wrap the overall composition in an appropriate `Link` or `Button` only when it genuinely navigates or acts, and ensure that control has an unambiguous name.

Image availability is internal fallback state, not an application-controlled value. There are no loading, disabled, invalid, error-callback, or read-only props. Breeze does not localise names. Initial derivation uppercases with JavaScript's standard Unicode behaviour and does not implement locale-specific name parsing.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not omit `name`, even when `src` is supplied.
- Do not place a separate `alt` on the internal image; it is not public anatomy.
- Do not use a tone to communicate status; use `Badge`, `Alert`, or visible text.
- Keep explicit initials brief. Avatar does not truncate an arbitrary `initials` string.
- Avatar does not fetch profiles, upload images, indicate presence, or own account menus.

## Related components

- Use `Logo` for the Motech Development brand.
- Use `IconTile` for non-identity visual markers.
- Use `Badge` beside identity content for a compact classification or status.
- Use `UserMenu` for the account-action pattern that may compose an Avatar.

foundation-badge--docs.name: Docs
foundation-badge--docs.title: Foundation/Badge
foundation-badge--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Badge.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact non-interactive status or classification label with semantic colour and emphasis."
/>

# Badge

`Badge` renders a compact, non-interactive status or classification label.

## Import

```tsx
import { Badge } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use for a short status, category, count label, or classification adjacent to primary content.
- Choose `variant` for meaning and `appearance` for the amount of visual emphasis.
- Keep the label concise enough to scan as metadata.

## When not to use

- Use `Button`, `ToggleButton`, or `Tag` when the element is interactive.
- Use `Alert` for a message that needs explanatory content or announcement semantics.
- Use `Typography` for sentences and supporting prose.
- Use `Meter` or `ProgressBar` for a measurable value rather than encoding progress as badge colour.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Badge, Inline, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ItemStatus() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Typography as="span">Quarterly report</Typography>
      <Badge variant="success">Complete</Badge>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.VariantsAndAppearances} />

## Variants

| Variant     | Intended use                                                          |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `primary`   | Current or product-primary classification.                            |
| `secondary` | Neutral metadata; the default.                                        |
| `success`   | Successful or complete state.                                         |
| `danger`    | Failed, destructive, or high-severity state.                          |
| `warning`   | State needing attention or caution.                                   |
| `info`      | Informational state.                                                  |
| `light`     | Low-emphasis label on an ordinary or dark-enough contrasting context. |
| `dark`      | Dark high-contrast label on a light context.                          |

The visible text must state the meaning; never rely on colour alone. Use one variant consistently for the same state within a product flow.

## Appearances

`subtle` is the default and works for most metadata. `solid` has the highest filled emphasis. `outline` uses a transparent background with a semantic border. `ghost` removes the visible border and background for the lightest treatment. Appearance does not change semantics or announce content.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## State, accessibility, and behaviour

Badge forwards relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, `data-*`, and event-independent text attributes. Arbitrary inline `style` is not a public styling contract.

Badge is a plain non-interactive span. It has no keyboard behaviour and no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, invalid, loading, empty, error, or read-only state. Empty badges provide no useful information and should not be rendered.

Dynamic Badge text is not automatically announced. If a change requires immediate assistive-technology attention, use the appropriate `Alert`, `Toast`, or application-owned live-region pattern rather than adding live semantics indiscriminately.

Keep wording understandable when colours are unavailable or forced-colour mode changes presentation. Badge content follows provider direction but is not translated or formatted by the component.

## Styling and composition

Badge has a canonical compact height, rounded geometry, display font, and content padding. Use `className` for placement in a parent layout, not to invent new variants, sizes, or shapes. Long content wraps, but a label that becomes a sentence belongs in Typography or Alert.

Icons may accompany text when they reinforce meaning, but keep them decorative and retain a visible label. An icon-only Badge is generally ambiguous.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not attach click handlers or present Badge as a control.
- Do not use `danger`, `warning`, or `success` without explicit wording.
- Do not put lengthy explanations inside the compact label.
- Do not use appearance as a state model; it controls emphasis only.
- Badge does not own counts, status transitions, or announcements.

## Related components

- Use `Tag` for an individual interactive or removable item.
- Use `Alert` for explanatory feedback.
- Use `IconTile` for a larger visual marker.
- Use `Typography` for ordinary metadata and prose.
foundation-badge--docs.summary: Compact non-interactive status or classification label with semantic colour and emphasis.

primitives-navigation-breadcrumbs--docs.title: Primitives/Navigation/Breadcrumbs
primitives-navigation-breadcrumbs--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Breadcrumbs.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="An ordered, labelled hierarchy of navigable ancestors ending in the current page."
/>

# Breadcrumbs

`Breadcrumbs` shows a user's position in a hierarchy and provides links back to its ancestors.

## Import

```tsx
import { Breadcrumbs } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use breadcrumbs for pages nested several levels deep when the hierarchy helps users orient themselves. Breadcrumbs supplement rather than replace primary navigation.

## When not to use

Prefer `NavigationList` for persistent destinations, `Tabs` for switching peer views, and a simple `Link` back to the previous step for a short linear task.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Breadcrumbs } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Breadcrumbs.Root aria-label="Breadcrumb">
  <Breadcrumbs.Item id="home" href="/">
    Home
  </Breadcrumbs.Item>
  <Breadcrumbs.Item id="settings" href="/settings">
    Settings
  </Breadcrumbs.Item>
  <Breadcrumbs.Item id="profile">Profile</Breadcrumbs.Item>
</Breadcrumbs.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.RoutingAndExtremes} />

## Anatomy and routing

| Part               | Purpose                                                                   |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Breadcrumbs.Root` | Renders the semantic ordered trail and applies group-wide disabled state. |
| `Breadcrumbs.Item` | Renders one keyed ancestor link or the non-link current location.         |

Give `Root` an accessible name such as `aria-label="Breadcrumb"`. Every `Item` requires a stable `id: string | number`. An item with `href` is a link; omit `href` only from the final current item, which receives `aria-current="page"`.

Local `/path` links use the optional `BreezeProvider` router adapter. External URLs and native anchor behaviour remain browser-owned. `disabled` on `Root` disables navigable crumbs; `disabled` on an item disables only that link. Breadcrumbs have no controlled, read-only, loading, invalid, empty, or error state. Do not render an empty trail.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and styling

Relevant ordered-list and list-item attributes, including `aria-*` and `data-*`, are supported. Inline `style` and conflicting collection identifiers are intentionally excluded.

## Accessibility, internationalisation, and limitations

Links use ordinary Tab navigation and Enter activation. Keep labels short but meaningful outside the trail, preserve hierarchy order, and never make the current item a link to itself. Long translated labels wrap; do not truncate the only identifying text. Separators are visual and generated by Breeze. Logical layout follows the provider direction.

Common mistakes include omitting the root label, supplying `href` on the current page, using array positions as persistent ids, and using breadcrumbs for workflow progress. Related components: use `Stepper` for progress, `NavigationList` for primary navigation, and `Link` for a standalone route.
primitives-navigation-breadcrumbs--docs.summary: An ordered, labelled hierarchy of navigable ancestors ending in the current page.

actions-button--docs.name: Docs
actions-button--docs.title: Actions/Button
actions-button--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Button.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A semantic application action with canonical emphasis, sizing, and accessible progress states."
/>

# Button

`Button` performs an application action through pointer or keyboard activation.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Button` to submit a form or change application state. Use `Link` for ordinary navigation, `LinkButton` when a destination needs button emphasis, `IconButton` for a compact icon-only action, and `ToggleButton` for a persistent on/off choice.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SaveButton() {
  return <Button onAction={() => saveChanges()}>Save changes</Button>;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Activation} />

## Emphasis and size

Choose `appearance` for emphasis and `variant` for meaning. `solid` is the default primary action; `subtle`, `outline`, and `ghost` progressively reduce emphasis. `text` is the lowest-emphasis button treatment and remains an action, never navigation. Semantic variants are `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, and `dark`. Avoid relying on colour alone; use clear action text.

Sizes are `sm`, `md`, and `lg`. Prefer `md`; use `sm` only in space-constrained contexts that retain an adequate target, and `lg` for deliberately prominent actions.

```tsx
<Button appearance="outline" variant="danger" onAction={removeItem}>
  Remove item
</Button>
```

## Activation, forms, and state

`onAction` runs once for a semantic activation, independent of whether the user clicked, touched, or used the keyboard. Use it instead of `onClick`. `type` is deliberately limited to `button` and `submit`, and defaults to the safe non-submitting `button`.

`disabled` prevents interaction. `loading` prevents activation while preserving focusability, sets `aria-busy`, and adds an announced in-progress state while retaining the visible label. Keep the label stable, such as “Save changes”, or use a short progressive label such as “Saving”. Errors and validation remain owned by the surrounding form or feedback component; `Button` has no invalid, error, read-only, controlled, or empty state.

<Canvas of={Stories.States} />

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard behaviour

Relevant native button attributes such as `name`, `value`, `form`, `autoFocus`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Breeze owns `onClick`, `disabled`, `type`, `aria-busy`, `className`, and inline styling through the API above.

Use a concise visible label; when an icon accompanies text, keep the icon decorative. `Enter` and `Space` activate the button when enabled, with browser and React Aria press semantics preventing duplicate callbacks. Focus remains visibly indicated. Do not remove focus styles or encode meaning using variant alone. Labels may expand after translation, so allow action layouts to wrap rather than truncating essential text.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Button` to navigate or pass an anchor destination; use `Link` or `LinkButton`.
- Do not attach both `onAction` and a native click handler.
- Do not put more than one primary solid action in the same decision group without a clear hierarchy.
- Do not replace a useful label with only an icon; use `IconButton` with `aria-label`.

## Related components

Use `LinkButton` for destinations, `IconButton` for icon-only actions, `ToggleButton` for persistent two-state actions, `ButtonGroup` for grouped actions, and `FormActions` for canonical form placement.
actions-button--docs.summary: A semantic application action with canonical emphasis, sizing, and accessible progress states.

date-and-time-calendar--docs.name: Docs
date-and-time-calendar--docs.title: Date and Time/Calendar
date-and-time-calendar--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Calendar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Always-visible locale-aware calendar for selecting one stable YYYY-MM-DD date, with compound navigation and grid parts, date constraints, and controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only state."
/>

# Calendar

`Calendar` presents an always-visible, keyboard-navigable month grid for selecting one calendar date.

## Import

```tsx
import { Calendar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Calendar` when the month grid should remain visible or is central to the task.

## When not to use

Use `DatePicker` for a compact labelled form control, `DateField` for direct segmented entry, and `RangeCalendar` for an inclusive start and end date. A calendar is not a replacement for a labelled form field when space or form conventions call for one.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Calendar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Calendar.Root defaultValue="2026-07-20">
  <Calendar.Header>
    <Calendar.PreviousButton />
    <Calendar.Heading />
    <Calendar.NextButton />
  </Calendar.Header>
  <Calendar.Grid />
</Calendar.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.KeyboardSelection} />

## Anatomy

| Part                      | Purpose                                                             |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Calendar.Root`           | Owns selection, visible month, constraints and calendar semantics.  |
| `Calendar.Header`         | Lays out the navigation controls and current-month heading.         |
| `Calendar.PreviousButton` | Moves to the previous calendar page.                                |
| `Calendar.Heading`        | Supplies the locale-formatted visible month heading and grid label. |
| `Calendar.NextButton`     | Moves to the next calendar page.                                    |
| `Calendar.Grid`           | Renders weekday headings and the complete date-cell grid.           |

Keep the navigation parts in `Header` and `Grid` inside `Root`. Breeze owns cells, selected styling and unavailable-date semantics; applications do not render individual days.

## Selection and constraints

The public value is a `YYYY-MM-DD` string such as `'2026-07-20'`, or `null` when empty. Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled selection, `value` with `onChange` for controlled selection, or `value` with `readOnly` for immutable selection. `onChange` receives the newly selected stable date or `null`.

`minValue` and `maxValue` bound selection. `isDateUnavailable(date)` receives the same stable format and disables a specific date when it returns `true`. `invalid` announces that the current selection is unacceptable but does not render an error message; place application-owned explanatory text next to the calendar. `disabled` prevents focus and selection. `visibleMonths` displays adjacent months but does not change the single-date value model. There is no loading state.

## Keyboard, labelling and locale

- Arrow keys move by day or week according to locale direction. Home and End move within a week; Page Up and Page Down move by month, with the platform modifier moving by a larger period.
- Enter or Space selects the focused available date. The previous and next buttons are separately tabbable and operable with Enter or Space.
- `Heading` gives the grid its accessible visible-period label. Keep it in the composition even if surrounding text also describes the calendar.
- Weekday names, week boundaries, date announcements and month headings follow the `BreezeProvider` locale. Logical navigation and arrow icons adapt to right-to-left direction.
- `weekdayStyle="short"` is the default; use `narrow` only when space is genuinely constrained because one-letter labels can be ambiguous.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass date objects or locale-formatted strings, render your own day buttons inside `Grid`, or hide unavailable dates instead of disabling them. Avoid large `visibleMonths` values in narrow layouts. Use `RangeCalendar` for a period, `DatePicker` for a compact popup, and `FormattedDateTime` for read-only prose.
date-and-time-calendar--docs.summary: Always-visible locale-aware calendar for selecting one stable YYYY-MM-DD date, with compound navigation and grid parts, date constraints, and controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only state.

data-display-card--docs.name: Docs
data-display-card--docs.title: Data Display/Card
data-display-card--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Card.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Semantic compound card for grouping one related content summary with optional heading, body, and footer regions."
/>

# Card

`Card` groups one self-contained content summary in a semantic article.

```tsx
import { Button, Card } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a card when a heading, supporting details, and related actions should read as one article.

## When not to use

Use [Surface](?path=/docs/layout-surface--docs) for visual containment without article semantics, [List](?path=/docs/data-display-list--docs) for a sequence, or [GridList](?path=/docs/collections-gridlist--docs) when items are selectable or actionable.

Do not turn an entire card into an improvised clickable container. Put a `Link`, `LinkButton`, or `Button` in the appropriate region, and preserve independent semantics for every action.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Button, Card, LinkButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

interface SummaryCardProps {
  onDismiss: () => void;
}

export function SummaryCard({ onDismiss }: SummaryCardProps) {
  return (
    <Card.Root>
      <Card.Header>
        <Card.Title>Release summary</Card.Title>
        <Card.Description>Updated a few moments ago</Card.Description>
      </Card.Header>
      <Card.Body>Three changes are ready for review.</Card.Body>
      <Card.Footer>
        <LinkButton href="/releases/current">Open details</LinkButton>
        <Button variant="secondary" onAction={onDismiss}>
          Dismiss
        </Button>
      </Card.Footer>
    </Card.Root>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Complete} />

## Anatomy

- `Card.Root` renders the required semantic `article` container.
- `Card.Header` renders a `header` for the title and optional description or controls.
- `Card.Title` renders the canonical `h3` heading.
- `Card.Description` renders supporting title context as a paragraph.
- `Card.Body` renders the primary content `div`.
- `Card.Footer` renders a `footer` for actions or supporting metadata.

`Header`, `Description`, `Body`, and `Footer` are optional. Keep the remaining parts in the order above. A body-only card is valid.

## Accessibility and behaviour

Cards add no keyboard interaction, selection, loading, disabled, invalid, or error state. Interactive descendants retain their own keyboard behaviour. Ensure heading levels remain logical in the surrounding page: `Card.Title` is always `h3`, so use a different semantic composition when that level is wrong. Translate all application-owned text; logical spacing supports left-to-right and right-to-left direction.

Every part supports the relevant native element attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; inline `style` is intentionally excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not omit a useful visible title merely to simplify the surface.
- Do not nest unrelated subjects in one card.
- Do not put footer actions outside `Card.Footer` when they belong to the article.
- Do not rely on colour or the border alone to communicate state.

## Related components

[Surface](?path=/docs/layout-surface--docs) provides non-semantic containment. [MetricCard](?path=/docs/patterns-data-metriccard--docs) presents a named measurement. [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs) communicates persistent feedback.
data-display-card--docs.summary: Semantic compound card for grouping one related content summary with optional heading, body, and footer regions.

layout-center--docs.name: Docs
layout-center--docs.title: Layout/Center
layout-center--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Center.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A neutral layout that centres one composition on both axes."
/>

# Center

`Center` centres its content horizontally and vertically, optionally within at least the viewport height.

## Import

```tsx
import { Center } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Center` for a bounded empty, loading, sign-in, or status composition that should sit on both axes. Use `Container` to centre and bound page width without vertical centring, or `Stack align="center"` when only cross-axis alignment is needed.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  Center,
  Spinner,
  Stack,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function LoadingView() {
  return (
    <Center minHeight="screen">
      <Stack align="center" gap="sm">
        <Spinner label="Loading results" />
        <Typography>Loading results…</Typography>
      </Stack>
    </Center>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Content} />

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and responsive behaviour

Relevant native `div` attributes including `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is not. `Center` has no callbacks or controlled, disabled, invalid, read-only, loading, empty, or error state of its own.

The wrapper is a neutral `div` with no role, focus, label, or keyboard behaviour. Give loading and status content its own accessible semantics. `minHeight="screen"` is a minimum rather than a fixed height, so zoomed, translated, or error content can grow and remain reachable. Centring is unchanged in right-to-left direction.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Center` to hide overflowing content or force a fixed viewport height.
- Do not centre long prose, dense forms, or whole application pages when start alignment is easier to scan.
- Do not assume it supplies live-region semantics for loading or errors.

## Related components

Use `StatePanel` for a standard empty/error composition, `Spinner` for announced progress, `Stack` inside the centred region, and `Container` for normal page gutters.
layout-center--docs.summary: A neutral layout that centres one composition on both axes.

selection-checkbox--docs.name: Docs
selection-checkbox--docs.title: Selection/Checkbox
selection-checkbox--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Checkbox.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible Boolean or indeterminate selection with a visible label, native checkbox form participation, and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, required, and invalid states."
/>

# Checkbox

`Checkbox` lets a user independently select or clear a Boolean choice and can present an application-owned indeterminate state.

## Import

```tsx
import { Checkbox } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a checkbox for an independent option or acknowledgement. Use `CheckboxGroup` for several related values, `Switch` for a setting that takes effect immediately, and `RadioGroup` when exactly one option may be selected. Do not use a checkbox as a button or as a substitute for expanding content.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Checkbox } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Checkbox.Root name="updates" value="email">
  <Checkbox.Control>
    <Checkbox.Indicator />
    <Checkbox.Label>Email updates</Checkbox.Label>
  </Checkbox.Control>
  <Checkbox.Description>You can change this later.</Checkbox.Description>
</Checkbox.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy

| Part                   | Purpose                                                                       |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Checkbox.Root`        | Owns Boolean state, validation, and the hidden native checkbox used by forms. |
| `Checkbox.Control`     | Native label and pointer, touch, and keyboard target.                         |
| `Checkbox.Indicator`   | Decorative selected or indeterminate mark.                                    |
| `Checkbox.Label`       | Persistent visible checkbox name inside `Control`.                            |
| `Checkbox.Description` | Supporting guidance associated with the checkbox.                             |
| `Checkbox.Error`       | Validation message associated while invalid.                                  |

Keep `Indicator` and `Label` inside `Control`; keep all parts inside `Root`. The indicator is automatically hidden from assistive technology because native checkbox state provides the meaning.

## Selection and form behaviour

Use `defaultSelected` for uncontrolled state, `selected` with `onChange` for controlled state, or `selected` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onChange` receives the next `boolean`. `indeterminate` is a visual and semantic mixed state controlled by the application; it does not add a third submitted value and user activation resolves it to a Boolean selection.

When selected, the hidden native checkbox submits `name=value`; `value` defaults to `'on'`. It submits nothing when unselected. `form` can associate it with an external form. `disabled` prevents focus and selection and excludes native submission. `readOnly` prevents changes while preserving the controlled value. `required` and `invalid` expose validation semantics; render a specific `Error`. Loading is application-owned.

## Keyboard and accessibility

- Tab focuses the checkbox; Space toggles it. Clicking the label toggles it.
- Use a short, affirmative `Label` that makes selected and unselected states understandable.
- Do not communicate state only through the indicator colour or shape.
- Use `Description` for consequences or additional context. For consent, avoid preselecting the checkbox.
- `sm`, `md` (default), and `lg` apply to `Indicator`; preserve a sufficiently large `Control` target.

The label and guidance accept Unicode and inherit surrounding direction. The visual indicator stays before the label in logical reading order.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not put the indicator outside `Control`, attach click handlers to the decorative indicator, treat indeterminate as stored selection, or use `disabled` to represent read-only information. Use `CheckboxGroup` for a named set, `Switch` for an immediate setting, and `RadioGroup` for exclusive selection.
selection-checkbox--docs.summary: Accessible Boolean or indeterminate selection with a visible label, native checkbox form participation, and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, required, and invalid states.

selection-checkboxgroup--docs.name: Docs
selection-checkboxgroup--docs.title: Selection/CheckboxGroup
selection-checkboxgroup--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './CheckboxGroup.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled multiple selection over composed Checkbox items with string-array values, native form naming, orientation, and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, required, and invalid states."
/>

# CheckboxGroup

`CheckboxGroup` coordinates a labelled set of `Checkbox` items where zero, one, or several string values may be selected.

## Import

```tsx
import { Checkbox, CheckboxGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for related multiple-choice options that should all remain visible. Use a standalone `Checkbox` for one independent choice, `RadioGroup` for exactly one value, or `TagGroup` for a compact interactive collection. Do not use it for a long or virtualised collection; consider `ListBox` with multiple selection.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Checkbox, CheckboxGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<CheckboxGroup.Root name="channels" defaultSelection={['email']}>
  <CheckboxGroup.Label>Notification channels</CheckboxGroup.Label>
  <Checkbox.Root value="email">
    <Checkbox.Control>
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
      <Checkbox.Label>Email</Checkbox.Label>
    </Checkbox.Control>
  </Checkbox.Root>
  <Checkbox.Root value="sms">
    <Checkbox.Control>
      <Checkbox.Indicator />
      <Checkbox.Label>Text message</Checkbox.Label>
    </Checkbox.Control>
  </Checkbox.Root>
</CheckboxGroup.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy and composition

| Part                        | Purpose                                                                     |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `CheckboxGroup.Root`        | Owns group selection, validation, orientation, and shared native form name. |
| `CheckboxGroup.Label`       | Persistent accessible name for the entire set.                              |
| `Checkbox.Root`             | One option; its `value` becomes the group’s string selection value.         |
| `CheckboxGroup.Description` | Group-level supporting guidance.                                            |
| `CheckboxGroup.Error`       | Group-level validation feedback while invalid.                              |

The group does not provide an item part: compose public `Checkbox` parts directly. Put selection state and `name` on `CheckboxGroup.Root`, and give each child `Checkbox.Root` a unique string `value`.

## Selection and state

Use `defaultSelection: string[]` for uncontrolled state, `selection` with `onSelectionChange` for controlled state, or `selection` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onSelectionChange` receives the complete next `string[]`, not a delta or DOM event. The default is an empty array.

`disabled` prevents all option interaction; an individual `Checkbox.Root disabled` disables only that option. `readOnly` preserves focusable values but prevents changes. `required` means at least one option is required. Set `invalid` and render `Error` after validation. Empty option sets and loading are application concerns; do not render an unlabelled empty group.

`orientation` is `vertical` by default and may be `horizontal`. Horizontal layout wraps and keeps group label, description, and error on their own rows. Prefer vertical layout for long labels or more than a few choices.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

- Tab visits each enabled checkbox; Space toggles the focused option. Checkboxes do not use arrow-key roving focus.
- `CheckboxGroup.Label` names the set; every `Checkbox.Label` must still name its option.
- Keep option values stable and distinct from their translated visible labels.
- In right-to-left contexts, layout and wrapping follow the surrounding `dir`; selection values remain the authored strings.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not control each checkbox separately while also controlling the group, use translated labels as persistent values, omit the group label, or expect arrow keys to move between checkboxes. Use `RadioGroup` for one value, `ListBox multiple` for a large selectable collection, and `Fieldset` for several different controls that form a native group.
selection-checkboxgroup--docs.summary: Labelled multiple selection over composed Checkbox items with string-array values, native form naming, orientation, and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, required, and invalid states.

collections-combobox--docs.name: Docs
collections-combobox--docs.title: Collections/ComboBox
collections-combobox--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ComboBox.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Editable keyed option selection with explicit selection-only or free-form modes, separately controllable input and selection state, asynchronous status parts, and accessible popup behaviour."
/>

# ComboBox

`ComboBox` combines editable text with a popup option collection for filtering a known set or explicitly accepting free-form text.

## Import

```tsx
import { ComboBox } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Selection-only mode is the default: the committed value must match an option. Set `allowsCustomValue` only when arbitrary text is a valid domain value.

## When not to use

Prefer `Select` when filtering is unnecessary, `SearchField` when text submits a query rather than selecting a value, and `TextField` when no suggestion collection exists.

## Basic selection-only example

```tsx
import { ComboBox } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<ComboBox.Root name="location">
  <ComboBox.Label>Location</ComboBox.Label>
  <ComboBox.Group>
    <ComboBox.Input placeholder="Choose a location" />
    <ComboBox.Trigger />
  </ComboBox.Group>
  <ComboBox.Popover>
    <ComboBox.ListBox>
      <ComboBox.Item id="north" textValue="North">
        North
      </ComboBox.Item>
      <ComboBox.Item id="south" textValue="South">
        South
      </ComboBox.Item>
    </ComboBox.ListBox>
  </ComboBox.Popover>
</ComboBox.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SelectionOnly} />

## Anatomy

| Part                    | Purpose                                                                |
| ----------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ComboBox.Root`         | Owns input text, selected key, mode, validation, and form integration. |
| `ComboBox.Label`        | Persistently names the editable input.                                 |
| `ComboBox.Group`        | Visually joins input and trigger and synchronises size.                |
| `ComboBox.Input`        | Displays editable filter or free-form text.                            |
| `ComboBox.Trigger`      | Opens and closes the option popup; defaults to a chevron.              |
| `ComboBox.Popover`      | Positions popup content relative to the control.                       |
| `ComboBox.ListBox`      | Renders static or typed generic options and empty content.             |
| `ComboBox.Item`         | One option with a stable string or number key and `textValue`.         |
| `ComboBox.Loading`      | Polite status for application-owned asynchronous option loading.       |
| `ComboBox.OptionsError` | Assertive alert for application-owned option loading failure.          |
| `ComboBox.Description`  | Supporting input guidance.                                             |
| `ComboBox.Error`        | Field validation feedback while invalid.                               |

Keep `Input` and `Trigger` inside `Group`, and `ListBox` inside `Popover`. `Loading` and `OptionsError` describe option retrieval; `Error` describes invalid field value.

## Selection, input, and free-form commits

Selection and displayed text are separate state channels. `selection` is a `string | number | null`; `inputValue` is a string. Each may be uncontrolled with `defaultSelection` or `defaultInputValue`, or controlled with its matching callback.

```tsx
<ComboBox.Root
  allowsCustomValue
  inputValue={query}
  onInputChange={setQuery}
  selection={selectedKey}
  onSelectionChange={setSelectedKey}
  onCommit={(value) => saveFreeFormValue(value)}
>
  {/* compound parts */}
</ComboBox.Root>
```

In free-form mode, `onCommit` receives the current input string when Enter is pressed without a highlighted option. Selecting an option still calls `onSelectionChange`. Do not infer the selected record from `inputValue`; use the stable selection key. Read-only state requires controlled input text and selection as appropriate and forbids change callbacks.

Static and dynamic collections follow the Breeze collection contract: dynamic items require stable `id` keys and a child renderer. `emptyContent` defaults to `'No options'`; set it to `null` only when another explicit status part communicates the state. The component does not fetch, debounce, cancel, or cache data.

## States and size

`disabled` prevents input, focus, and selection. `readOnly` preserves immutable controlled text/selection. `invalid`, `required`, `Description`, and `Error` provide field validation. Use `Loading` while options are pending and `OptionsError` when loading fails; keep stale options only if product behaviour makes that explicit.

`Group` applies `sm`, `md` (default), or `lg` to the input and trigger. Standalone `Input` and `Trigger` also accept size, but the containing group takes precedence.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

- Tab focuses the input, then the trigger. Arrow Down opens the popup and moves through options; Escape closes it.
- Enter selects the highlighted option. In free-form mode only, Enter with no active option invokes `onCommit`.
- Typeahead/filtering uses option `textValue`; provide literal plain text for visual or complex option content.
- Always provide `Label`. Loading is announced politely; loading failure is announced assertively. Avoid duplicating those messages in another live region.
- The popup follows surrounding direction. Keep keys locale-independent; translate visible text, empty content, and status messages.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not enable custom values merely to permit temporary filter text, conflate `inputValue` with the selected key, use unstable item IDs, fetch data inside render, or show `OptionsError` for ordinary field validation. Use `Select` for compact known options, `SearchField` for search results, and `ListBox` for persistent collection selection.
collections-combobox--docs.summary: Editable keyed option selection with explicit selection-only or free-form modes, separately controllable input and selection state, asynchronous status parts, and accessible popup behaviour.
