progression-stepper--docs.name: Docs
progression-stepper--docs.title: Progression/Stepper
progression-stepper--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Stepper.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible ordered progression showing complete, current, and upcoming application-owned stages."
/>

# Stepper

`Stepper` describes named stages in a process as an ordered list with one current-step marker.

```tsx
import { Stepper } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { CheckIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use a stepper when people benefit from knowing their position in a small, stable multi-stage process.

## When not to use

Use [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs) for continuous task completion, [Tabs](?path=/docs/primitives-navigation-tabs--docs) for switching peer views, and [Breadcrumbs](?path=/docs/primitives-navigation-breadcrumbs--docs) for location in a hierarchy.

Stepper is descriptive, not navigation: it does not change pages, validate data, or persist completion.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Stepper } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { CheckIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function SetupProgress() {
  return (
    <Stepper.Root aria-label="Account setup">
      <Stepper.Item
        indicator={<CheckIcon />}
        status="complete"
        title="Details"
      />
      <Stepper.Item indicator="2" status="current" title="Settings" />
      <Stepper.Item indicator="3" status="upcoming" title="Review" />
    </Stepper.Root>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Horizontal} />

## Anatomy

- `Stepper.Root` renders the ordered-list container and controls visual orientation.
- `Stepper.Item` renders one list item, its decorative indicator, visible title, and optional description.

## Status and orientation

Every item requires `status`: `complete`, `current`, or `upcoming`. `current` adds `aria-current="step"`. Keep exactly one current item during an active flow; the indicator is intentionally hidden from assistive technology, so the visible translated `title` and status semantics must carry meaning. `orientation` is `horizontal` by default and may be `vertical`; it changes layout, not reading order or keyboard behaviour.

<Canvas of={Stories.VerticalWithDescriptions} />

## Accessibility, state, and direction

Give the root an accessible name with `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` when surrounding context does not name it. Use meaningful text titles, not icon-only titles. Stepper has no focus management, keyboard interaction, callbacks, controlled/uncontrolled value, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state; the application derives statuses from its own state. Translate titles, descriptions, and the root name. Source order remains progression order in both directions.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not make list items clickable without real links or buttons.
- Do not mark multiple items current.
- Do not communicate completion only with indicator colour or an icon.
- Do not reorder items visually without changing their semantic source order.

## Related components

[ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), [Tabs](?path=/docs/primitives-navigation-tabs--docs), and [Breadcrumbs](?path=/docs/primitives-navigation-breadcrumbs--docs).
progression-stepper--docs.summary: Accessible ordered progression showing complete, current, and upcoming application-owned stages.

layout-surface--docs.name: Docs
layout-surface--docs.title: Layout/Surface
layout-surface--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Surface.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A canonical background, border, and responsive inset for grouped content."
/>

# Surface

`Surface` gives grouped content a canonical Breeze background, border treatment, and responsive inset.

## Import

```tsx
import { Surface } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Surface` as a low-level visual region when you need deliberate background, boundary, and padding choices.

## When not to use

Use `Card` when content has card anatomy and actions, or `Container` for page width and gutters. Avoid surfaces around every group: hierarchy should remain meaningful.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Stack, Surface, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function Summary() {
  return (
    <Surface as="section" aria-labelledby="summary-title" tone="subtle">
      <Stack gap="sm">
        <Typography id="summary-title" as="h2" level="heading-md">
          Summary
        </Typography>
        <Typography>Three items are ready.</Typography>
      </Stack>
    </Surface>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.TonesAndBorders} />

## Tone, border, and inset

`default` is the normal raised content surface, `subtle` creates quiet grouping, `canvas` matches the application canvas, and `inverse` is for content on the shell colour. Choose tone for hierarchy and contrast, not decoration. `border="strong"` increases boundary emphasis; `none` is appropriate only when surrounding contrast already defines the region.

`padding` accepts `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, or `page`, directly or as `{ base, sm?, md?, lg? }` at 681 px, 901 px, and 1181 px.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and semantics

Relevant native HTML attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is excluded. There is no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state.

Colour and borders add no accessible semantics. Choose `section` only for a labelled document section and `article` only for independently distributable content; otherwise retain `div`. Ensure custom content meets contrast requirements, particularly on `inverse`. `Surface` adds no keyboard or focus behaviour and works in both text directions.

## Common mistakes

- Do not select `section` or `article` solely for styling.
- Do not rely on tone alone to convey success, warning, or error state; use `Alert` where status semantics matter.
- Do not apply arbitrary background and padding classes when a public tone or spacing token expresses the design.

## Related components

Use `Card` for structured content, `Alert` for semantic feedback, `Container` for page bounds, and `Stack` or `Grid` for internal layout.
layout-surface--docs.summary: A canonical background, border, and responsive inset for grouped content.

  </Stack>
);

selection-switch--docs.name: Docs
selection-switch--docs.title: Selection/Switch
selection-switch--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Switch.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible compound on/off setting with visible label, native checkbox form participation, and controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, required, and invalid states."
/>

# Switch

`Switch` represents a Boolean setting whose on or off state usually takes effect immediately.

## Import

```tsx
import { Switch } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for persistent settings such as enabling notifications or a display preference. Prefer `Checkbox` for selection, acknowledgement, or a value submitted as part of a larger form. Do not use a switch to trigger a one-off action; use `Button`.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Switch } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Switch.Root defaultSelected name="updates">
  <Switch.Control>
    <Switch.Track>
      <Switch.Thumb />
    </Switch.Track>
    <Switch.Label>Email updates</Switch.Label>
  </Switch.Control>
  <Switch.Description>Changes take effect immediately.</Switch.Description>
</Switch.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndKeyboard} />

## Anatomy

| Part                 | Purpose                                                                      |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Switch.Root`        | Owns Boolean state, validation, and hidden native checkbox form integration. |
| `Switch.Control`     | Native label and complete pointer, touch, and keyboard target.               |
| `Switch.Track`       | Decorative on/off track.                                                     |
| `Switch.Thumb`       | Decorative moving thumb inside the track.                                    |
| `Switch.Label`       | Persistent visible name inside the control.                                  |
| `Switch.Description` | Supporting guidance associated with the switch.                              |
| `Switch.Error`       | Validation feedback associated while invalid.                                |

Keep `Thumb` inside `Track`, and `Track` plus `Label` inside `Control`. Track and thumb are hidden from assistive technology; native switch state carries the meaning.

## State, callbacks, and forms

Use `defaultSelected` for uncontrolled state, `selected` with `onChange` for controlled state, or `selected` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onChange` receives the complete next Boolean.

When on, the hidden native checkbox submits `name=value`; `value` defaults to `'on'`. It submits nothing when off. `form` associates it with an external form. `disabled` prevents focus and changes and removes normal submission. `readOnly` preserves controlled state without changes. `required` and `invalid` are available, though switches rarely make sense as required acknowledgements—prefer `Checkbox` for consent.

Loading is application-owned. If a change is saved asynchronously, keep the switch’s state truthful, communicate progress separately, and handle failure without silently reverting.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

- Tab focuses the switch; Space toggles it. Clicking the visible label also toggles it.
- The `Label` should describe the setting, not instruct “turn on”. State is announced separately.
- Do not add “on/off” text that conflicts with the native announced state.
- `Track` and `Thumb` each support `sm`, `md` (default), and `lg`; use the same size for both while retaining the control’s large hit target.
- Logical layout and thumb movement follow surrounding direction.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use Switch for submit-time choices, mismatch track and thumb sizes, put interactive content inside its label, or show stale optimistic state after a failed save. Use `Checkbox` for acknowledgement, `ToggleButton` for a pressed command, and `SegmentedControl` for mutually exclusive modes.

const StaticOrderedSections = () => (

    <Table.Footer id="footer-section">

collections-table--docs.name: Docs
collections-table--docs.title: Collections/Table
collections-table--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Table.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Responsive compound data table with ordered columns, keyed rows, selection, consumer-owned sorting, actions, grouped sections, loading, and optional virtualization."
/>

# Table

`Table` presents comparable records in ordered columns while coordinating responsive labels, row focus, selection, sorting, actions, and large collections.

```tsx
import { Table } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a table when people need to scan or compare the same attributes across records. Use [DescriptionList](?path=/docs/data-display-descriptionlist--docs) for the attributes of one record, [GridList](?path=/docs/collections-gridlist--docs) for rich selectable rows without aligned columns, and [List](?path=/docs/data-display-list--docs) for non-interactive sequences.

Avoid a table when each row has a different information structure or when a small-screen record cannot be understood from compact labels.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Table } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function RecordsTable() {
  return (
    <Table.Root aria-label="Records">
      <Table.Header>
        <Table.Column id="name" rowHeader>
          Name
        </Table.Column>
        <Table.Column id="state">State</Table.Column>
      </Table.Header>
      <Table.Body>
        <Table.Row id="alpha" textValue="Alpha Ready">
          <Table.Cell column="name">Alpha</Table.Cell>
          <Table.Cell column="state">Ready</Table.Cell>
        </Table.Row>
        <Table.Row id="beta" textValue="Beta In review">
          <Table.Cell column="name">Beta</Table.Cell>
          <Table.Cell column="state">In review</Table.Cell>
        </Table.Row>
      </Table.Body>
    </Table.Root>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ControlledSortingAndSelection} />

## Anatomy

- `Table.Root` owns table semantics, layout, responsive labels, row state, sorting, and virtualization.
- `Table.Header` renders the ordered column-heading section from static children or generic `items`.
- `Table.Column` renders one keyed heading and configures row-header, sorting, compact label, alignment, and width behaviour.
- `Table.Body` renders one ordered row section and its empty content.
- `Table.Row` renders one keyed data or section row, with optional action and tone.
- `Table.Cell` associates one cell with a stable column key.
- `Table.Disclosure` renders the canonical bare arrow cell for an actionable row.
- `Table.Footer` renders an optional ordered footer row section.
- `Table.LoadMore` renders a visible loading row and intersection-driven request sentinel.

Keep `Header`, each `Body`, and optional `Footer` in meaningful source order. Every data row must contain exactly one cell for every visible column, in the same order, even after conditional column changes.

## Keys and collection content

Column, row, and section identifiers are stable `string | number` keys. Do not use array positions or translated labels. Each `Table.Cell column` must exactly match its `Table.Column id`. `Table.Row textValue` is required for typeahead and a plain-text accessible representation. Provide `Table.Column textValue` and `Table.Cell textValue` when visible content is not plain text; Breeze does not derive accessible or compact labels from arbitrary React nodes.

`Table.Header`, `Table.Body`, and `Table.Footer` accept either authored children or generic `items` plus a renderer. Dynamic items must contain a stable `id`. `id` on each section is optional but recommended for stable measurement when sections can reorder or virtualization is enabled.

## Responsive layouts and columns

`layout="responsive"` is the default. It presents compact labelled records below the Breeze small breakpoint and native table columns at wider sizes. `responsiveGrid` keeps the same compact treatment but uses explicit equal grid tracks at wider sizes. `grid` keeps CSS grid structure at all sizes. Use `responsiveGrid` or `grid` only when equal tracks and aligned custom geometry fit the content.

`Table.Column compactLabel` defaults to `true`; a literal string heading becomes the compact label. When the heading contains formatting, icons, or other React nodes, provide explicit `textValue`. Set `compactLabel` to `false` for a disclosure or control column that should not label compact cells. `align` is `start`, `center`, or `end` and defaults to `start`. `width` is `auto`, `control`, or `icon` and defaults to `auto`. `boundary="strong"` adds a stronger lower edge; `none` is the default.

<Canvas of={Stories.ResponsiveGroupedSections} />

## Selection, sorting, and actions

Selection is disabled until you supply selection state, a selection callback, or `multiple`. Uncontrolled selection uses `defaultSelection`; controlled selection requires `selection` and `onSelectionChange`; immutable selection requires `selection`, `readOnly`, and no selection callback. Values are `Array<string | number>` or `'all'`. `multiple` defaults to `false`, and `onSelectionChange` receives the complete next selection.

Sorting never sorts consumer data. Mark a column `sortable`, then either use `defaultSort` for retained uncontrolled sort metadata or pass controlled `sort` with `onSortChange`. A `TableSort` is `{ column: string | number, direction: 'ascending' | 'descending' }`. Reorder the supplied rows in response. `onSortChange` receives the next complete descriptor. Read-only mode suppresses sort changes as well as selection changes.

`Table.Row onAction` receives that row's stable key. Add `Table.Disclosure column="actions"` to an actionable row when a visual arrow is useful, and define a matching column with `compactLabel={false}` and `width="icon"`. `position="overlay"` is the disclosure default for compact records; `flow` keeps it in an explicit track. Buttons and links inside cells remain independent controls.

```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import {
  Table,
  type CollectionSelection,
  type TableSort,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ControlledTable() {
  const [selection, setSelection] = useState<CollectionSelection>([]);
  const [sort, setSort] = useState<TableSort>({
    column: 'name',
    direction: 'ascending',
  });

  return (
    <Table.Root
      aria-label="Records"
      onSelectionChange={setSelection}
      onSortChange={setSort}
      selection={selection}
      sort={sort}
    >
      <Table.Header>
        <Table.Column id="name" rowHeader sortable>
          Name
        </Table.Column>
      </Table.Header>
      <Table.Body emptyContent="No records">
        <Table.Row id="alpha" textValue="Alpha">
          <Table.Cell column="name">Alpha</Table.Cell>
        </Table.Row>
      </Table.Body>
    </Table.Root>
  );
}
```

## Row presentation and states

`Table.Row presentation="data"` and `tone="default"` are defaults. Use `tone="muted"` for domain-neutral de-emphasis, not disabled state. `presentation="section"` creates a grouped section row; it is structure inside an ordered body, not a substitute for column headings. `disabled` or `disabledKeys` prevent row focus, selection, and actions.

`Table.Body emptyContent` defaults to `No rows`; supply translated copy. `Table.LoadMore onLoadMore` requests more consumer-owned rows. Set `loading` for the duration of the request to show loading content and suppress duplicates; `offset` defaults to `1` viewport proportion. Table has no built-in error row: render application feedback and a real retry action without breaking the header/body structure.

## Virtualization

Pass `virtualization` only for a large collection. Both modes require `viewportHeight` in pixels.

| Mode                   | Required                      | Optional defaults and behaviour                                           |
| ---------------------- | ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `{ mode: 'fixed' }`    | `rowHeight`, `viewportHeight` | One authoritative row height; `gap` defaults to `0`.                      |
| `{ mode: 'variable' }` | `viewportHeight`              | `estimatedRowHeight` defaults to `48`; live row measurements override it. |

Both modes accept `overscan` (default `0` beyond the engine buffer), `loadingRowHeight` (default `48`), and `estimatedSectionHeight`. Keep section and row keys stable. Test responsive content at the configured height; clipping a variable-height row by using fixed mode is a data-loss bug.

<Canvas of={Stories.VariableVirtualizationAndLoading} />

## Keyboard, accessibility, and internationalization

Every table needs an accessible name through visible context, `aria-label`, or `aria-labelledby`. Mark the primary identifying column `rowHeader` so assistive technology can announce records while navigating cells. Arrow keys move through the table, Home and End move within row/collection boundaries, and typeahead uses row `textValue`. Standard selection keys follow the current mode; Enter or equivalent activation invokes actionable rows. Sortable headings are keyboard activatable. Embedded controls remain in the Tab sequence.

Translate the table name, headings, empty/loading/error copy, and text values. Format dates and numbers before rendering cells. `start`/`end`, logical disclosure positioning, and inherited direction adapt to RTL; keep semantic column and row source order stable rather than manually reversing it.

Parts support relevant native table attributes, `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and typed refs. Click, value, children, alignment, role, and inline-style props owned by the compound API are intentionally excluded where applicable.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not mismatch cell `column` keys, heading order, or conditional visible columns.
- Do not assume `onSortChange` sorts rows for you.
- Do not use unstable row keys or omit `textValue`.
- Do not attach generic click handlers to rows; use `onAction` or cell controls.
- Do not use fixed virtualization for wrapping rows.
- Do not hide meaningful headings with `compactLabel={false}`.

## Related components

[GridList](?path=/docs/collections-gridlist--docs), [DescriptionList](?path=/docs/data-display-descriptionlist--docs), [List](?path=/docs/data-display-list--docs), and [Pagination](?path=/docs/patterns-navigation-pagination--docs).

primitives-navigation-tabs--docs.name: Docs
primitives-navigation-tabs--docs.title: Primitives/Navigation/Tabs
primitives-navigation-tabs--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Tabs.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible keyed tab labels and associated panels with controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, and orientation-aware behaviour."
/>

# Tabs

`Tabs` switches between peer panels within the same page without changing the user's task context.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tabs } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use tabs for a small, stable set of related views whose labels fit comfortably. Use `NavigationList` when each destination is a route, `Accordion` when users benefit from reading several sections together, and `ToggleGroup` for compact display-mode choices.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tabs } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Tabs.Root defaultValue="summary">
  <Tabs.List aria-label="Record details">
    <Tabs.Tab id="summary">Summary</Tabs.Tab>
    <Tabs.Tab id="history">History</Tabs.Tab>
  </Tabs.List>
  <Tabs.Panels>
    <Tabs.Panel id="summary">Summary content</Tabs.Panel>
    <Tabs.Panel id="history">History content</Tabs.Panel>
  </Tabs.Panels>
</Tabs.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ControlledKeyboardAndDisabled} />

## Anatomy

| Part          | Purpose                                                |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `Tabs.Root`   | Owns keyed selection, disabled state, and orientation. |
| `Tabs.List`   | Accessible list of tab labels.                         |
| `Tabs.Tab`    | One keyed selectable label.                            |
| `Tabs.Panels` | Optional wrapper coordinating panel transitions.       |
| `Tabs.Panel`  | Content whose `id` matches one `Tab`.                  |

Every tab and panel pair must use the same stable `string | number` id. `Panels` is optional, but useful when panels are grouped; do not place unrelated content between a tab and its associated panel structure.

## Selection, states, and orientation

Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled selection, `value` with `onChange` for controlled selection, or `value` with `readOnly` for immutable selection. `onChange` receives the complete next key, not an event. `disabled` on `Root` disables all tabs; `Tab.disabled` disables one and removes it from selection navigation. `forceMount` keeps an inactive panel mounted while React Aria preserves inactive semantics; use it only when local state or expensive setup must survive.

Horizontal orientation is the default and uses Left/Right keys. Vertical orientation uses Up/Down keys and places labels alongside panels. Tabs have no built-in loading, invalid, empty, or error state; keep a stable labelled tab set and put relevant feedback inside its panel.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and styling

All parts accept `className`, relevant `aria-*` and `data-*` attributes, and a ref to their rendered `div`. Inline styles and native click/change handlers are excluded where Breeze owns behaviour.

## Accessibility, direction, and mistakes

Label `Tabs.List`. Tab moves into the active tab; arrow keys move and activate selection, Home and End reach the first and last enabled tab, and Tab then moves into panel content. React Aria mirrors horizontal arrow behaviour for RTL. Translate visible labels without changing ids and allow labels to wrap only when the resulting layout remains understandable.

Do not use tabs as route links, mismatch ids, hide critical errors in an inactive panel without an announcement, or create so many tabs that labels become an overflow menu. Related components: `NavigationList`, `Accordion`, `Disclosure`, and `ToggleGroup`.

collections-tag--docs.name: Docs
collections-tag--docs.title: Collections/Tag
collections-tag--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Tag.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="One keyed focusable tag item for use in a TagGroup collection, with stable identity, text representation, disabled state, and optional semantic action."
/>

# Tag

`Tag` is one keyed interactive item for a `TagGroup`; the containing group supplies selection and removal behaviour.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Tag` inside `TagGroup.List` for compact labels that may be focused, selected, acted on, or removed. Prefer `Badge` for passive status or metadata, `Button` for an action, and `ListBox.Item` for a conventional option collection. A standalone Tag story demonstrates the item, but production composition should provide TagGroup context and an accessible group label.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TagGroup.Root>
  <TagGroup.Label>Topics</TagGroup.Label>
  <TagGroup.List>
    <Tag id="accessibility" textValue="Accessibility">
      Accessibility
    </Tag>
    <Tag id="design" textValue="Design systems">
      Design systems
    </Tag>
  </TagGroup.List>
</TagGroup.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Static} />

## Identity and behaviour

`id` is a required stable `string | number` used for focus, selection, actions, and removal. `textValue` is required plain text for typeahead and accessibility. Visible `children` may contain richer presentation, but must preserve the same meaning.

`disabled` prevents focus, selection, action, and removal. `onAction` receives this tag’s key when its semantic action is invoked. Selection and removal callbacks belong to `TagGroup.Root`; Tag has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, loading, invalid, or error state of its own.

## Keyboard and accessibility

Keyboard behaviour is coordinated by `TagGroup`: arrow keys move between tags, and selection, action, or removal keys depend on enabled group behaviours. Do not put links, buttons, or other interactive descendants inside `Tag`; use the tag’s semantic action or a separate control. Keep `id` independent of translated content and provide an accurate `textValue` for every locale.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use array indexes as IDs, omit `textValue`, nest interactive controls, or treat a Tag as a passive badge. Use `TagGroup` for collection state, `Badge` for status, `Button` for a command, and `ListBox` for a larger option collection.
collections-tag--docs.summary: One keyed focusable tag item for use in a TagGroup collection, with stable identity, text representation, disabled state, and optional semantic action.

collections-taggroup--docs.name: Docs
collections-taggroup--docs.title: Collections/TagGroup
collections-taggroup--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TagGroup.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled static or typed tag collection with optional multiple selection, disabled keys, controlled, uncontrolled and read-only state, application-owned removal, and an explicit empty state."
/>

# TagGroup

`TagGroup` coordinates a labelled collection of keyed `Tag` items with optional multiple selection and application-owned removal.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for compact chosen filters, categories, recipients, or similar items where selection or removal is meaningful. Prefer `Badge` plus a layout primitive for passive metadata, `CheckboxGroup` for form choices with persistent checkbox affordances, and `ListBox` for a larger selectable collection. Do not use tags as a substitute for navigation links.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TagGroup.Root onRemove={(keys) => removeTopics(keys)}>
  <TagGroup.Label>Topics</TagGroup.Label>
  <TagGroup.List>
    <Tag id="design" textValue="Design">
      Design
    </Tag>
    <Tag id="research" textValue="Research">
      Research
    </Tag>
  </TagGroup.List>
  <TagGroup.Description>Delete removes the focused topic.</TagGroup.Description>
</TagGroup.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.StaticKeyboard} />

## Anatomy

| Part                   | Purpose                                                                                                   |
| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TagGroup.Root`        | Owns collection focus, optional multiple selection, disabled keys, read-only state, and removal callback. |
| `TagGroup.Label`       | Persistent accessible name for the tag collection.                                                        |
| `TagGroup.List`        | Renders static or typed generic tags and the empty state.                                                 |
| `Tag`                  | One keyed item with plain-text representation, disabled state, and optional action.                       |
| `TagGroup.Description` | Supporting guidance associated with the collection.                                                       |

There is no group error part or invalid/required API. Validate the application concept in surrounding form composition if necessary.

## Collections, selection, and removal

Static lists contain authored `Tag` children. Dynamic lists accept `items`, each with stable `id: string | number`, and a child renderer.

```tsx
<TagGroup.List items={topics}>
  {(topic) => (
    <Tag id={topic.id} textValue={topic.name}>
      {topic.name}
    </Tag>
  )}
</TagGroup.List>
```

Selection is opt-in. Supplying `defaultSelection`, `selection`, or `onSelectionChange` enables multiple selection. Values use `CollectionKey[] | 'all'`. Use `defaultSelection` for uncontrolled state, `selection` with `onSelectionChange` for controlled state, or `selection` with `readOnly` for immutable state. The callback receives the complete next selection.

`onRemove` receives all stable keys removed through keyboard interaction. It does not mutate consumer data; remove the matching items in application state. It is unavailable in read-only mode. `disabledKeys` prevents matching tags from focus, selection, action, and removal. `emptyContent` defaults to `'No tags'`. Loading and loading errors are application-owned.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

- Tab enters and leaves the tag collection; arrow keys move between enabled tags.
- Selection keys follow tag-group conventions when selection is enabled. Delete or Backspace requests removal when `onRemove` is supplied.
- Disabled tags are skipped. Read-only mode prevents selection changes and removal.
- Provide `Label`, an accurate `textValue` for every Tag, and `Description` when removal or selection is not obvious.
- Keep keys locale-independent. Translated labels, descriptions, and empty content may change without changing selection.
- Wrapping and arrow interpretation follow surrounding direction.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not assume `onRemove` changes the items, enable selection accidentally with an unused callback, use translated labels as keys, omit `textValue`, or put buttons inside tags. Use `Badge` for passive metadata, `CheckboxGroup` for form choices, and `ListBox` for a larger or virtualised collection.
collections-taggroup--docs.summary: Labelled static or typed tag collection with optional multiple selection, disabled keys, controlled, uncontrolled and read-only state, application-owned removal, and an explicit empty state.

fields-textarea--docs.name: Docs
fields-textarea--docs.title: Fields/TextArea
fields-textarea--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TextArea.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled multiline text entry with controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation, sizing, and native textarea behaviour."
/>

# TextArea

`TextArea` collects free-form content that can span several lines while keeping label, guidance, and validation explicitly composed.

## Import

```tsx
import { TextArea } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for notes, summaries, descriptions, and other paragraph-length content. Prefer `TextField` for a short single-line value. Do not use a textarea for structured rich text, code editing, or a choice from known values; use a purpose-built editor or selection control.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { TextArea } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TextArea.Root>
  <TextArea.Label>Notes</TextArea.Label>
  <TextArea.Control name="notes" rows={5} />
  <TextArea.Description>Use plain text.</TextArea.Description>
</TextArea.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy

| Part                   | Purpose                                                  |
| ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TextArea.Root`        | Owns string value and shared field state.                |
| `TextArea.Label`       | Persistently names the textarea.                         |
| `TextArea.Control`     | Renders the native multiline textarea.                   |
| `TextArea.Description` | Associates supporting guidance with the control.         |
| `TextArea.Error`       | Associates validation feedback when the root is invalid. |

All parts belong inside `Root`. `TextArea` intentionally shares its state and guidance contract with `TextField`; the editable part is named `Control` rather than `Input`.

## Value and states

Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled state, `value` with `onChange` for controlled state, or `value` with `readOnly` for an intentionally immutable value. `onChange` receives the complete next string. Empty content is `''`.

`disabled` prevents focus and edits. `readOnly` preserves focus, selection, copying, and form participation without allowing edits. `invalid` associates `Error`; `required` exposes native required semantics. Loading and character limits are application concerns: use native `maxLength` where it is a genuine constraint and keep any count in `Description`.

## Size and resizing

`Control` supports `size="sm" | "md" | "lg"`; `md` is the default. Its minimum height changes with size and users may resize it vertically. Use `rows` to express the expected amount of content, but do not force a fixed height that prevents reviewing entered text.

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

- Tab moves focus; Enter inserts a new line. Normal platform editing, selection, clipboard, and undo commands remain available.
- Always provide a persistent `Label`. A placeholder is a hint, not an accessible name.
- Put pre-entry guidance in `Description`; put a specific recovery instruction in `Error` and set `invalid`.
- Do not intercept Enter to submit the surrounding form unless the product clearly provides another way to enter line breaks.
- Unicode text and the surrounding `dir` are respected. Use `dir="auto"` for user-authored text whose direction is unknown.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not put state props on `Control`, remove resize access without an equivalent, or use the placeholder as the only label. Use `TextField` for a line, `SearchField` for a query, and `FormSection` or `Fieldset` to organise multiple related fields.

stories.name: Read Only And States
stories.snippet:
const ReadOnlyAndStates = () => (
  <StoryConstraint size="bounded">
    <Stack gap="lg">
      <TextField.Root readOnly value="Immutable value">
        <TextField.Label>Read-only</TextField.Label>
        <TextField.Input />
      </TextField.Root>
      <TextField.Root disabled defaultValue="Unavailable">
        <TextField.Label>Disabled</TextField.Label>
        <TextField.Input size="sm" />
      </TextField.Root>
      <TextField.Root invalid required>
        <TextField.Label>Required reference</TextField.Label>
        <TextField.Input size="lg" />
        <TextField.Error>Enter a reference.</TextField.Error>
      </TextField.Root>
    </Stack>
  </StoryConstraint>
);
stories.description: Compares immutable, disabled, and required-invalid inputs across all control sizes so consumers can distinguish their semantics and presentation.
stories.summary: read-only disabled and invalid text-field states

stories.name: Keyboard And Native Form
stories.snippet:
const KeyboardAndNativeForm = () => (
  <form onSubmit={(event) => event.preventDefault()}>
    <TextField.Root defaultValue="BRZ-3" required>
      <TextField.Label>Project code</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input autoComplete="off" name="projectCode" />
      <TextField.Description>
        Submitted through the native form.
      </TextField.Description>
    </TextField.Root>
  </form>
);
stories.description: Verifies Tab reaches the labelled input while demonstrating a stable name, required state, initial value, and native form participation.
stories.summary: keyboard focus with native form field integration

stories.name: Content Extreme
stories.snippet:
const ContentExtreme = () => (
  <StoryConstraint size="narrow-control">
    <TextField.Root defaultValue="A very long field value that remains usable in a constrained application column">
      <TextField.Label>
        A persistent label with unusually detailed wording
      </TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input />
      <TextField.Description>
        Long guidance wraps without changing the field's accessible
        relationships.
      </TextField.Description>
    </TextField.Root>
  </StoryConstraint>
);
stories.description: Places unusually long label, value, and description content in a narrow column to verify wrapping without breaking accessible relationships.
stories.summary: long single-line field content in a narrow column

fields-textfield--docs.name: Docs
fields-textfield--docs.title: Fields/TextField
fields-textfield--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TextField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled single-line text entry with controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation, and native form behaviour."
/>

# TextField

`TextField` is the standard compound control for collecting one line of text with a persistent label and associated guidance.

## Import

```tsx
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `TextField` for names, references, email addresses, telephone numbers, URLs, passwords, and other short free-form values. Use `TextArea` for paragraphs, `NumberField` for quantities that need numeric stepping or locale-aware formatting, and `SearchField` for a query that can be submitted or cleared.

Do not use a text field when the valid values form a known set. Prefer `Select` for compact selection, `RadioGroup` when all choices should remain visible, or `ComboBox` when users need to filter a large set.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ContactField() {
  return (
    <TextField.Root>
      <TextField.Label>Email address</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input name="email" type="email" autoComplete="email" />
      <TextField.Description>Used for service updates.</TextField.Description>
    </TextField.Root>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy

| Part                    | Purpose                                                                                      |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TextField.Root`        | Owns value, disabled, read-only, required, and invalid state and associates the field parts. |
| `TextField.Label`       | Persistently names the input. It renders a native label.                                     |
| `TextField.Input`       | Renders the native single-line input.                                                        |
| `TextField.Description` | Provides supporting guidance associated with the input.                                      |
| `TextField.Error`       | Provides the accessible validation message when `Root` is invalid.                           |

Keep all parts inside `Root`. A visible `Label` is the preferred accessible name; use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` on `Input` only when a visible label is genuinely unsuitable.

## Value and state

Uncontrolled fields use `defaultValue`; controlled fields pair `value` with `onChange`; immutable controlled fields pair `value` with `readOnly` and deliberately omit `onChange`.

```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ControlledReferenceField() {
  const [reference, setReference] = useState('BRZ-3');

  return (
    <TextField.Root value={reference} onChange={setReference}>
      <TextField.Label>Reference</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input name="reference" />
    </TextField.Root>
  );
}
```

`onChange` receives the complete next string, not a DOM change event. `disabled` prevents focus and editing. `readOnly` keeps the value focusable and available to forms but prevents edits. `required` exposes required semantics; application validation remains responsible for setting `invalid` and rendering a helpful `Error`. An empty value is the empty string. Loading is application-owned; disable the field only when interaction must stop.

## Input types, size, and native attributes

`Input` supports `type="text"`, `"email"`, `"password"`, `"tel"`, and `"url"`, and sizes `sm`, `md` (default), and `lg`. It accepts relevant native input attributes such as `name`, `placeholder`, `autoComplete`, `inputMode`, `pattern`, `minLength`, `maxLength`, and `aria-*`. Put value and state props on `Root`, not `Input`.

## Keyboard and accessibility

- Tab moves focus to and from the native input; normal platform text-editing, selection, clipboard, and undo commands are preserved.
- The label must describe the expected value, not merely repeat the placeholder. Placeholders disappear and are not labels.
- Use `Description` for format guidance before an error occurs. Use `Error` for a specific recovery instruction and set `invalid` on `Root`.
- Keep `required` consistent with the application’s validation rules. Do not encode requiredness through an asterisk alone.
- Use an appropriate `autoComplete` token and `type`; browsers and assistive technology can then offer better input behaviour.

Text values are Unicode strings and support left-to-right and right-to-left content. The component follows the surrounding `dir`; use `dir="auto"` on the input when user-authored content may have an unknown direction.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not pass `value`, `defaultValue`, `disabled`, `readOnly`, `required`, or `onChange` to `Input`; these belong to `Root`.
- Do not switch between controlled and uncontrolled state during the component’s lifetime.
- Do not hide a persistent label to save space; concise labels improve scanning and speech-input use.
- Do not use `invalid` merely to style a warning. It communicates a real validation failure.

## Related components

Use `TextArea` for multiline content, `SearchField` for submitted queries, `NumberField` for numeric values, `PasswordField` for a revealable password pattern, and `InputGroup` to visually combine an input with a unit or related action.

date-and-time-timefield--docs.name: Docs
date-and-time-timefield--docs.title: Date and Time/TimeField
date-and-time-timefield--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TimeField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Locale-aware segmented entry for one wall-clock time, using stable HH:mm or HH:mm:ss values with native form participation and controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only state."
/>

# TimeField

`TimeField` edits a wall-clock time through locale-aware segments without attaching it to a date or time zone.

## Import

```tsx
import { TimeField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `TimeField` for a time of day whose date and zone are separate business concepts. Use `DateTimePicker` for a complete instant with an explicit offset, or `FormattedDateTime` to display rather than edit a date-time. Do not use it for elapsed durations, countdowns or free-form time text.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { TimeField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TimeField.Root
  defaultValue="14:30"
  minValue="09:00"
  maxValue="17:00"
  name="startTime"
>
  <TimeField.Label>Start time</TimeField.Label>
  <TimeField.Input />
  <TimeField.Description>Between 09:00 and 17:00.</TimeField.Description>
</TimeField.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SegmentKeyboard} />

## Anatomy

| Part                    | Purpose                                                                     |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TimeField.Root`        | Owns stable time state, boundaries, validation and native form integration. |
| `TimeField.Label`       | Visible accessible name for the segmented input.                            |
| `TimeField.Input`       | Locale-aware editable hour, minute and optional second segments.            |
| `TimeField.Description` | Supporting guidance associated with the field.                              |
| `TimeField.Error`       | Validation message associated with an invalid field.                        |

## Values, precision and state

Use 24-hour stable strings: `'14:30'` for minute precision or `'14:30:45'` for second precision. An empty value is `null`. The visual control may present a locale-appropriate 12- or 24-hour cycle, but callbacks always use the stable 24-hour format. Seconds appear when any of `value`, `defaultValue`, `minValue` or `maxValue` contains three colon-separated segments; keep precision consistent across those props.

Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled state, `value` with `onChange` for controlled state, or `value` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onChange` receives the next complete stable time or `null`. `minValue` and `maxValue` are inclusive. `disabled` prevents focus and changes; `readOnly` preserves an inspectable controlled value; `invalid` and `required` expose validation state. Empty and loading behaviour remain application-owned.

When `name` is supplied, the hidden native time input submits the stable value. Use `form` for an external form and `inputRef` only when direct access to that hidden input is necessary.

## Keyboard, accessibility and internationalisation

- Tab enters and leaves the segmented control. Arrow keys move between or increment segments; numeric typing edits the focused segment, and Backspace or Delete clears editable content.
- Always render `Label`. Use `Description` for constraints and `Error` for a correction when `invalid` is true.
- Locale controls visual segment order, hour cycle and spoken labels. Provider direction controls logical layout.
- Do not localise the stored value or infer a time zone from the displayed hour. Daylight-saving rules do not apply because this component has no date or zone.
- Do not communicate required, invalid or disabled state only through appearance.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a JavaScript `Date`, an ISO date-time, a locale-formatted time or an offset such as `+01:00`. Do not mix minute and second precision accidentally. Use `DateField` for a separate calendar date, `DateTimePicker` for one date-time instant, and `RelativeTime` for phrases such as “in 2 hours”.

feedback-toastregion--docs.name: Docs
feedback-toastregion--docs.title: Feedback/ToastRegion
feedback-toastregion--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ToastRegion.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Advanced additional presentation of the nearest BreezeProvider toast queue with a separately translated accessible label."
/>

# ToastRegion

`ToastRegion` renders an additional labelled presentation of the nearest `BreezeProvider` toast queue. The provider already renders the primary region automatically.

```tsx
import { ToastRegion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Most applications should not render this component. Use it only when an advanced layout deliberately needs another presentation of the same provider-owned queue and the accessibility impact has been tested. Use [`useToast`](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs) to add and dismiss notifications; use standalone `Toast` when the application owns an independent notification source.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { ToastRegion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SecondaryNotificationRegion() {
  return <ToastRegion label="Secondary notifications" />;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AdditionalRegion} />

## Queue and lifecycle behaviour

The region reads the existing queue from the nearest provider; it does not create another controller, queue, limit, or notification lifecycle. `BreezeProvider toastLimit` still controls the maximum visible queued toasts. Rendering outside `BreezeProvider` throws an error.

Because an additional region presents the same queue, careless use can duplicate visible content, announcements, focus destinations, and actions. Do not add one merely to reposition the primary stack. Prefer configuring the provider portal host and application layout where possible.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalization

`label` names the region and must distinguish it from the provider-owned primary region; when omitted it uses the provider's translated `messages.notifications` value. Translate an explicit label. Queued toasts retain their alert-dialog semantics, F6 region navigation, Tab and Shift+Tab action navigation, Escape dismissal, timeout behaviour, and close/action labels.

The region is fixed at the logical block start and inline end, so it follows the provider's left-to-right or right-to-left direction. The provider-owned portal carries `lang` and `dir`. Test focus movement and announcements with every region present.

ToastRegion has no controlled/uncontrolled value, callback, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state. These belong to the shared queue and application notification content.

`ToastRegion` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not render `ToastRegion` outside `BreezeProvider`.
- Do not render an additional region without a tested layout and accessibility reason.
- Do not give two regions the same accessible label.
- Do not expect a region to create an independent queue or change `toastLimit`.
- Do not use it as a replacement for `useToast`.

## Related components

[Toast and useToast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs), [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs), and [BreezeProvider](?path=/docs/foundation-breezeprovider--docs).

actions-toolbar--docs.name: Docs
actions-toolbar--docs.title: Actions/Toolbar
actions-toolbar--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Toolbar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A labelled collection of related controls with orientation-aware arrow-key navigation."
/>

# Toolbar

`Toolbar` groups related interactive controls under toolbar semantics and coordinates arrow-key navigation.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, IconButton, Toolbar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { DeleteIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use `Toolbar` for a compact collection of related commands, such as editor or item actions. Use `Inline` for layout without toolbar semantics, `ToggleGroup` for related selected values, `Menu` when actions should be collapsed, or `ButtonGroup` for a small canonical action arrangement.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, IconButton, Toolbar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { DeleteIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function ItemToolbar() {
  return (
    <Toolbar aria-label="Item actions">
      <Button>Edit details</Button>
      <IconButton aria-label="Delete item" variant="danger">
        <DeleteIcon />
      </IconButton>
    </Toolbar>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Horizontal} />

## Composition and orientation

The `Toolbar` root owns the toolbar role, label, layout, and focus coordination. Compose it from public interactive controls such as `Button`, `IconButton`, `ToggleButton`, or links. The root has no public compound parts. Use `horizontal` for ordinary action rows and `vertical` only when the visual arrangement and expected arrow axis are genuinely vertical.

Disabled state belongs to each child control; `Toolbar` has no group-wide disabled, loading, invalid, error, empty, selection, controlled, or read-only state. An empty toolbar provides no value and should not render.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, keyboard, and direction

Other relevant native `div` attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Children, class, and inline style are constrained by the public API.

Every toolbar needs an accessible name, especially when more than one exists on a page. `Tab` enters and leaves the toolbar; the arrow keys for its orientation move focus between enabled controls. Horizontal arrow behaviour follows the interface direction, while vertical toolbars use Up and Down. `Enter` or `Space` activates the focused button according to that child’s semantics. Each icon-only child still requires its own `aria-label`.

Keep related controls together, order them logically in the DOM, and translate both visible and accessible labels. Do not use toolbar semantics solely to obtain a flex row.

## Common mistakes

- Do not omit the toolbar’s accessible label.
- Do not place unrelated controls in one toolbar.
- Do not add manual arrow-key handlers or roving `tabIndex` values to children.
- Do not use `Inline` when users need toolbar navigation, or `Toolbar` when layout alone is required.
- Do not render an empty toolbar while actions are loading; render appropriate progress or omit the region.

## Related components

Use `Inline` for neutral layout, `ToggleGroup` for selection, `Menu` for collapsed actions, `ButtonGroup` for an opinionated action pattern, and `Separator` to divide meaningful command clusters where needed.
actions-toolbar--docs.summary: A labelled collection of related controls with orientation-aware arrow-key navigation.

primitives-overlays-tooltip--docs.name: Docs
primitives-overlays-tooltip--docs.title: Primitives/Overlays/Tooltip
primitives-overlays-tooltip--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Tooltip.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Concise non-interactive descriptive help revealed from a button or named focusable icon by hover, focus, or touch."
/>

# Tooltip

`Tooltip` gives an existing trigger concise, non-essential descriptive help without adding an action surface.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tooltip } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { WarningIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use it to clarify a control whose visible label cannot carry a short secondary explanation. Use persistent text for essential instructions, `Popover` for interactive content, and `VisuallyHidden` to supply an accessible label that does not need a visual popup. Tooltips never replace a control's accessible name.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tooltip } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Tooltip.Root>
  <Tooltip.Trigger appearance="outline">Export</Tooltip.Trigger>
  <Tooltip.Content>Download the current results</Tooltip.Content>
</Tooltip.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.KeyboardHoverAndTouch} />

## Anatomy

| Part                  | Purpose                                                               |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Tooltip.Root`        | Coordinates delayed hover, focus, touch, and controlled open state.   |
| `Tooltip.Trigger`     | Full public `Button` used as an action described by the tooltip.      |
| `Tooltip.IconTrigger` | Compact focusable informational icon with a required accessible name. |
| `Tooltip.Content`     | Concise, non-interactive descriptive popup.                           |

Use exactly one trigger part. `IconTrigger.children` should be a decorative icon and `aria-label` names the icon itself. The tooltip adds description; it does not supply that name.

```tsx
<Tooltip.Root>
  <Tooltip.IconTrigger aria-label="Warning information" variant="danger">
    <WarningIcon aria-hidden="true" />
  </Tooltip.IconTrigger>
  <Tooltip.Content variant="danger">
    This item requires attention
  </Tooltip.Content>
</Tooltip.Root>
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SemanticIconTrigger} />

## State, timing, and presentation

Use `defaultOpen` for uncontrolled state, `open` with `onOpenChange` for controlled state, or `open` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onOpenChange` receives the complete next Boolean. Opening delay defaults to `700`ms and closing delay to `0`ms; use shorter delays only where repeated inspection warrants it.

Content placement defaults to `top`; supported values are `top`, `bottom`, `left`, `right`, and top/bottom start/end. Collision handling may change the final position. `offset` defaults to `6` pixels. `variant` is `secondary` by default and may be `primary` or `danger`; keep icon and content variants aligned and never use colour as the only meaning.

Trigger inherits Button disabled and loading behaviour. A disabled native button cannot receive focus or hover consistently, so essential disabled explanations should remain visible. Tooltip itself has no invalid, empty, error, or loading state and Content must not contain interactive elements.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and styling

IconTrigger and Content accept relevant `aria-*`, `data-*`, className, and element refs. Inline styling and conflicting interaction handlers are excluded.

## Keyboard, touch, internationalisation, and mistakes

Keyboard focus and pointer hover reveal Content after the delay; Escape dismisses it. Touch behaviour is provided by React Aria without making Content interactive. Keep content to a short phrase, translate it independently of stable application state, and allow wrapping. Start/end placement follows direction.

Do not put links, buttons, form controls, rich errors, or essential instructions in Content; do not use an unnamed IconTrigger; do not repeat the visible trigger label without adding information. Related components: `Popover`, `VisuallyHidden`, `IconButton`, and `Alert`.

foundation-typography--docs.name: Docs
foundation-typography--docs.title: Foundation/Typography
foundation-typography--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Typography.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Semantic non-interactive text with an independent visual level and constrained presentation options."
/>

# Typography

`Typography` renders non-interactive text while keeping the document element independent from its visual level.

## Import

```tsx
import { Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use for headings, paragraphs, labels in non-form display content, summaries, metrics, and inline text.
- Choose `as` for semantic document structure, then use `level` only when appearance must differ.
- Use `tabularNumbers` for changing values that should remain visually aligned.

## When not to use

- Use `Link` for navigation and `Button` for actions; do not add click behaviour to Typography.
- Use the label, description, error, or legend part of a field component for form labelling so the accessible relationship is preserved.
- Use layout primitives such as `Stack`, `Inline`, and `Container` for spacing and positioning instead of treating Typography as a layout API.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Stack, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function Introduction() {
  return (
    <Stack gap="sm">
      <Typography as="h2">Project summary</Typography>
      <Typography colour="muted">
        Review the latest activity and outstanding actions.
      </Typography>
    </Stack>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SemanticIndependence} />

## Semantics and visual levels

`as` controls the rendered HTML element. It defaults to `p`. Heading elements automatically use their matching visual level; `strong` and `dt` default to `label`; all other elements default to `body`. An explicit `level` overrides only the appearance, never the element.

Maintain a logical heading hierarchy. A visually smaller heading is still expressed with the correct `h1`–`h6` element:

```tsx
import { Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SubsectionHeading() {
  return (
    <Typography as="h3" level="h5">
      Contact preferences
    </Typography>
  );
}
```

Visual levels are `h1`, `h2`, `h3`, `h4`, `h5`, `h6`, `metric`, `summary`, `label`, and `body`. `metric` uses tabular numerals by design; `summary` is a responsive display treatment; `label` is bold display text.

## Colour, weight, and alignment

Colours are semantic tokens: `default`, `muted`, `primary`, `inverse`, `inverse-muted`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, and `info`. Use inverse colours only on a sufficiently dark surface. Colour cannot be the only way information is conveyed.

Weights are `regular`, `medium`, `semibold`, and `bold`; omitting `weight` preserves the selected level's canonical weight. `align="start"` and `align="end"` are direction-aware. When `align` is omitted, ordinary CSS inheritance applies.

## Overflow and rhythm

`truncate` limits content to one line with an ellipsis. `lineClamp` limits it to 2, 3, or 4 lines. Both require a constrained available width and hide overflow; ensure the full information is available elsewhere when the omitted text matters.

`gutterBottom` adds canonical level-appropriate bottom rhythm to block content. It is ignored for `span`, `strong`, `em`, and `small`. Prefer a `Stack` when spacing between several siblings because it expresses the relationship at the layout level.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## State, accessibility, and internationalisation

Relevant native HTML attributes, including `id`, `aria-*`, `data-*`, and event-independent text attributes, are forwarded. The conflicting native `align`, `color`, and `style` attributes are intentionally not public styling contracts.

Typography has no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, loading, invalid, or read-only state. Express those states on the owning interactive component and use Typography only for associated explanatory content.

It adds no keyboard behaviour. Native semantics come from `as`, so headings appear in assistive-technology navigation and `dt`/`dd` retain description-list meaning. Logical alignment follows the provider direction. Breeze does not transform, translate, or format the children; use the formatting components for locale-aware values.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not select `as` for appearance. A `div` styled as `h2` is not a heading.
- Do not use `level="label"` as a substitute for a form control's label part.
- Do not use `danger` colour alone to communicate an error.
- Do not clamp essential copy without another way to access it.
- General margins, padding, width, positioning, and arbitrary inline styles are outside the API.

## Related components

- Use `Link` for inline destinations.
- Use `FormattedDateTime`, `FormattedList`, `FormattedNumber`, or `RelativeTime` for locale-aware text values.
- Use `Stack` or `Inline` to arrange multiple text elements.
- Use `Badge` for compact status or classification labels, not prose.
foundation-typography--docs.summary: Semantic non-interactive text with an independent visual level and constrained presentation options.

foundation-visuallyhidden--docs.name: Docs
foundation-visuallyhidden--docs.title: Foundation/VisuallyHidden
foundation-visuallyhidden--docs.content:
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './VisuallyHidden.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Visually concealed span whose content remains available to assistive technology."
/>

# VisuallyHidden

`VisuallyHidden` removes supporting content from visual layout while keeping it available to assistive technology.

## Import

```tsx
import { VisuallyHidden } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Add a concise accessible name when visible context and a decorative icon are sufficient for sighted users.
- Add non-visual clarification to otherwise ambiguous visible text.
- Supply an accessible table or collection label where a visible heading would be redundant in context.

## When not to use

- Prefer a visible label whenever space and design allow; visible instructions benefit more users.
- Use `Tooltip` for supplementary information that sighted pointer and keyboard users also need.
- Use `SkipLink` for a focusable bypass link; VisuallyHidden does not reveal content on focus.
- Do not use it to hide decorative content from assistive technology. Use `aria-hidden="true"` or the icon's decorative default instead.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Button, VisuallyHidden } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { MenuIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function NavigationButton() {
  return (
    <Button>
      <MenuIcon />
      <VisuallyHidden>Open navigation</VisuallyHidden>
    </Button>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AccessibleIconAction} />

## Anatomy and behaviour

VisuallyHidden renders one `span`. Its one-pixel clipped box remains in the accessibility tree but does not occupy ordinary visual layout. The component adds no roles, labels, relationships, live-region behaviour, or keyboard interaction of its own; those come from the parent semantic element and the hidden content.

The text becomes part of the accessible name when composed inside controls that derive their name from children. Test the final control by role and accessible name rather than assuming the relationship.

```tsx
import { Link, VisuallyHidden } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ReportLink() {
  return (
    <Link href="/reports/quarterly">
      Quarterly report
      <VisuallyHidden> (PDF, opens in the current tab)</VisuallyHidden>
    </Link>
  );
}
```

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and internationalisation

Relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `lang`, `dir`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are forwarded. Arbitrary inline `style` is intentionally excluded.

Write hidden text in the same plain language and locale as the control it describes. It follows the provider's direction and language unless a native `lang` or `dir` override is supplied for genuinely mixed-language content.

Do not place focusable controls inside VisuallyHidden. Keyboard users could focus an element they cannot see, and the component has no focus-reveal treatment. Do not hide essential instructions solely from sighted users or duplicate visible text word-for-word unless the duplicate is required for a specific semantic relationship.

VisuallyHidden has no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, loading, invalid, error, empty, or read-only state. Empty hidden content has no effect and should be omitted.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not use it as a general-purpose CSS hiding utility.
- Do not conceal validation errors or instructions that all users need.
- Do not put `Button`, `Link`, inputs, or other focusable descendants inside it.
- Do not override clipping, positioning, dimensions, or overflow through `className`.
- Hidden text may affect speech output and voice-control names even though it is not visible; keep it concise and consistent with visible wording.

## Related components

- Use `SkipLink` for a keyboard-visible bypass control.
- Use `Tooltip` for supplementary information available visually on hover and focus.
- Use icon `aria-label` when a meaningful standalone icon is the semantic image, rather than merely naming a parent control.
- Use visible field label and description parts for form guidance.
foundation-visuallyhidden--docs.summary: Visually concealed span whose content remains available to assistive technology.

stories.name: Left To Right
stories.snippet: const LeftToRight = () => <BreezeProvider direction={undefined} locale="en-GB"><Typography>Provider content</Typography></BreezeProvider>;
stories.description: Uses the required en-GB locale with inferred direction and verifies that the provider stamps matching language and left-to-right attributes on its root.
stories.summary: locale-inferred left-to-right provider environment

stories.name: Explicit Right To Left
stories.snippet: const ExplicitRightToLeft = () => <BreezeProvider direction="rtl" locale="en-GB"><Typography>Provider content</Typography></BreezeProvider>;
stories.description: Overrides direction to right-to-left while retaining English content so locale-sensitive behaviour and layout direction can be configured separately.
stories.summary: explicit right-to-left direction with an English locale
